Protestants add the doxology ("for thine is the kingdom, the power, and the glory…"); Catholics omit it from the prayer itself but the priest speaks it separately at Mass.

The Hail Mary

Hail Mary, full of grace, the Lord is with thee. Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus. Holy Mary, Mother of God, pray for us sinners, now and at the hour of our death. Amen.Used: the Rosary (50+ times per full rosary), the Angelus, personal devotion, moments of distress

The most distinctively Catholic prayer. The first half is biblical: "Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you" is Gabriel's greeting at the Annunciation (Luke 1:28), and "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb" is Elizabeth's greeting at the Visitation (Luke 1:42). The second half is the Church's addition (formalized by the 16th century): a request for Mary's intercession focused on two moments — "now" (whatever you're facing) and "the hour of our death" (the ultimate moment of vulnerability). Protestants object that praying to Mary violates the sole mediatorship of Christ (1 Timothy 2:5). Catholics respond that asking Mary to pray for you is intercessory prayer, not worship — functionally identical to asking a living friend to pray for you, except the friend happens to be in heaven.

The Glory Be (Doxology)

Glory be to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, as it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.Used: concludes each decade of the Rosary, concludes psalms in the Liturgy of the Hours, general praise

A Trinitarian praise formula dating to at least the 4th century. "As it was in the beginning" affirms that the Trinity is not a development but an eternal reality — God has always been Father, Son, and Spirit. The phrase "world without end" translates the Latin in saecula saeculorum ("unto the ages of ages").

The Apostles' Creed

I believe in God, the Father almighty, Creator of heaven and earth, and in Jesus Christ, his only Son, our Lord, who was conceived by the Holy Spirit, born of the Virgin Mary, suffered under Pontius Pilate, was crucified, died and was buried; he descended into hell; on the third day he rose again from the dead; he ascended into heaven, and is seated at the right hand of God the Father almighty; from there he shall come to judge the living and the dead. I believe in the Holy Spirit, the holy catholic Church, the communion of saints, the forgiveness of sins, the resurrection of the body, and life everlasting. Amen.Used: the Rosary, baptismal profession of faith, personal devotion, simpler alternative to the Nicene Creed

The older and simpler of the two great Christian creeds (the other being the Nicene Creed professed at Sunday Mass). Despite its name, it was not written by the apostles — the tradition that each apostle contributed one line is a medieval legend. The creed developed from the Old Roman baptismal formula (2nd–3rd century) and reached its current form by the 8th century. It is used in the Rosary (recited at the beginning), in baptismal ceremonies (the candidate professes it), and as a personal prayer. Its language is deliberately simple — no philosophical vocabulary like the Nicene Creed's homoousios — making it the most accessible summary of the Catholic faith. "He descended into hell" refers not to the hell of the damned but to the realm of the dead (the Sheol / Hades of the ancient world), where Christ proclaimed liberation to the righteous dead between his death and resurrection.

The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed)

I believe in one God, the Father almighty, maker of heaven and earth, of all things visible and invisible. I believe in one Lord Jesus Christ, the Only Begotten Son of God, born of the Father before all ages. God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, consubstantial with the Father; through him all things were made. For us men and for our salvation he came down from heaven, and by the Holy Spirit was incarnate of the Virgin Mary, and became man. For our sake he was crucified under Pontius Pilate, he suffered death and was buried, and rose again on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures. He ascended into heaven and is seated at the right hand of the Father. He will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead and his kingdom will have no end. I believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father and the Son, who with the Father and the Son is adored and glorified, who has spoken through the prophets. I believe in one, holy, catholic and apostolic Church. I confess one Baptism for the forgiveness of sins and I look forward to the resurrection of the dead and the life of the world to come. Amen.Used: every Sunday Mass and holy day of obligation — the central profession of faith of the universal Church

Produced by the Councils of Nicaea (325 AD) and Constantinople (381 AD) — hence its full name, the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed. Professed at every Sunday Mass worldwide, in every language, for over sixteen centuries. It is the doctrinal backbone of the Christian faith and the most carefully worded theological document in history: every phrase was contested, debated, and chosen with precise intention. What follows is a clause-by-clause guide to what you are actually saying when you profess it.

"I believe in one God"
The Greek original uses pisteuō — a verb of active trust and commitment, not passive intellectual assent. "I believe" is not "I think it's probably true" but "I entrust myself to." The "one" (unum) is the first anti-polytheist assertion: not a god among gods but the only God. The creed opens by staking out monotheism against every ancient and modern alternative.

"the Father almighty, maker of heaven and earth, of all things visible and invisible"
"Almighty" (pantokrator in Greek) means not merely that God can do anything but that God sustains all things in existence — the ruler of all, not just the powerful one. "Maker of heaven and earth" is the first anti-Gnostic clause: the physical world is not the work of an evil demiurge but of the one true God, and it is therefore good. "Visible and invisible" extends creation to include the angels — spiritual creatures made by God, good by nature, capable of free choice, some of whom fell.

"I believe in one Lord Jesus Christ, the Only Begotten Son of God, born of the Father before all ages"
"Lord" (Kyrios) is the same Greek word used in the Septuagint for YHWH — the divine name. Calling Jesus "Lord" is a divine title. "Only Begotten" (Greek monogenēs) distinguishes Christ's sonship from the adoptive sonship of all believers — he is Son by nature, not by grace. "Born before all ages" is the anti-Arian phrase: the Son did not begin to exist at the Incarnation; he exists eternally as the Father's Son. There was no time when the Son was not.

"God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, consubstantial with the Father"
The most theologically dense phrase in the creed — written specifically against Arius of Alexandria (d. 336), who taught that the Son was the highest created being, made by the Father, but not co-equal with him. "God from God" — the Son's divinity is derived from the Father's divinity but is not less than it. "Light from Light" — a flame lit from another flame is just as much fire as its source. "Begotten, not made" — the key distinction: to be made is to come into existence from nothing; to be begotten is to share the nature of the one who begets. Human fathers beget human sons; the divine Father begets a divine Son. "Consubstantial with the Father" (homoousios, "of one substance") — the single word that ended the Arian controversy and is the doctrinal foundation of Trinitarian Christianity. The difference between homoousios (same substance) and homoiousios (similar substance) is a single Greek letter — iota — and represents the difference between orthodox Christianity and a Christology in which Jesus is a demigod. This is why "not one iota of difference" has entered English as an idiom.

"through him all things were made"
The Son is not a creature but the agent of creation — echoing John 1:3 ("through him all things were made; without him nothing was made that has been made") and Colossians 1:16 ("in him all things were created"). The universe was made through the one who became flesh at the Incarnation. This is why the Incarnation is not a secondary event in the divine plan but its center: the creator entering his own creation.

"For us men and for our salvation he came down from heaven"
The Incarnation is purposive: not a philosophical demonstration of God's nature but a rescue mission. "For us" and "for our salvation" — the Incarnation is an act of divine love directed toward a specific goal. "Came down from heaven" uses spatial language for a non-spatial reality: the pre-existent Son entered creation, which is an act of condescension (Latin: condescensio, coming down — the theological term for God accommodating himself to human categories).

"and by the Holy Spirit was incarnate of the Virgin Mary, and became man"
Two assertions in one clause: the agent of the Incarnation (the Holy Spirit) and the human vessel (the Virgin Mary). The virginal conception is not incidental mythology but a theological statement: this child has no human father because his Father is God. The Holy Spirit's role at the Annunciation (Luke 1:35: "The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you") mirrors the Spirit's role at Creation (Genesis 1:2: "the Spirit of God was hovering over the waters") — the Incarnation is a new creation. "Became man" (enanthropēsanta) — the Greek emphasizes that the Son took on full humanity, not a human body with a divine soul substitute (the Apollinarian error, condemned 381 AD). At this line, the congregation bows — a physical acknowledgment of what is being said.

"For our sake he was crucified under Pontius Pilate, he suffered death and was buried"
The creed names a historical Roman official — Pontius Pilate, governor of Judea 26–36 AD — anchoring the crucifixion in datable history, not myth. This is the creed's anti-Docetist clause: he truly suffered and truly died. Docetism (from Greek dokein, to seem) taught that Christ only appeared to suffer because as God he could not really die. The creed insists on the reality of the suffering and burial — a dead body, wrapped in linen, placed in a tomb.

"and rose again on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures"
"In accordance with the Scriptures" — the resurrection was not a surprise but a fulfillment, the climax of everything the OT had been pointing toward (Psalm 16, Hosea 6:2, Jonah 1:17, Isaiah 53:10–11). Paul uses the exact same phrase in the oldest creedal formula in the NT (1 Corinthians 15:3–4), written within twenty years of the event: "that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures."

"He ascended into heaven and is seated at the right hand of the Father"
The Ascension (Acts 1:9–11) is not the disappearance of Christ but his glorification — the risen human nature of Christ taken permanently into the life of the Trinity. "Seated at the right hand" is royal language from Psalm 110:1 ("Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet") — the most quoted OT verse in the NT, applied to Christ's post-resurrection exaltation. Christ reigns now, from the right hand of the Father, as the one mediator between God and humanity.

"He will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead and his kingdom will have no end"
The Second Coming (Greek: parousia) is not optional eschatology but a central creedal affirmation. "In glory" — as opposed to the first coming in humility. "Judge the living and the dead" — the universal judgment of all persons who have ever lived. "His kingdom will have no end" — a deliberate echo of the Davidic covenant's "his throne will be established forever" (2 Samuel 7:16), fulfilling the angel Gabriel's words to Mary: "his kingdom will never end" (Luke 1:33).

"I believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father and the Son"
The Holy Spirit is described with three titles that define his divine status: "the Lord" (divine), "the giver of life" (the Spirit who hovered over the waters at creation and who gives new life in baptism — John 3:5–8), and the one who "proceeds from the Father and the Son" (the filioque). The phrase "and the Son" (filioque in Latin) was added by the Western Church — first at local Spanish councils in the 6th century, universally by the 11th century — and is rejected by the Eastern Orthodox as both a theological error and a procedural violation. It remains one of the primary causes of the 1054 Great Schism. The Eastern version reads "who proceeds from the Father" only.

"who with the Father and the Son is adored and glorified, who has spoken through the prophets"
The clearest anti-Macedonian clause: Macedonius of Constantinople (d. ~360) taught that the Holy Spirit was a created being, subordinate to Father and Son. The creed asserts equal divine worship and glory for the Spirit — co-equal in the Trinity. "Who has spoken through the prophets" grounds the Spirit's activity in OT history: the same Spirit who inspired Isaiah and Jeremiah is the same Spirit given at Pentecost and dwelling in the Church.

"I believe in one, holy, catholic and apostolic Church"
The four marks of the Church — one, holy, catholic, apostolic — have been debated since the Reformation. "One": the Church is not a federation of denominations but a single body with a single head. "Holy": not that all its members are saints but that God is its author and the sacraments are his effective instruments. "Catholic" (Greek katholikos: universal): addressed to all peoples, in all times, in all places — the opposite of a sect. "Apostolic": founded on the apostles, governed by their successors (the bishops), faithful to their teaching.

"I confess one Baptism for the forgiveness of sins"
Baptism is singular and unrepeatable — not because water is magic but because it enacts a death and resurrection that, by its nature, happens once (Romans 6:3–4). It forgives "sins" — plural, including both original sin (the inherited disorder of human nature) and all personal sins committed before baptism. The Catholic Church recognizes as valid any baptism performed with water and the Trinitarian formula ("in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit"), even outside its own walls.

"and I look forward to the resurrection of the dead and the life of the world to come. Amen."
The creed ends not with death but with anticipation: "I look forward to" (prosdokō in Greek — I await eagerly, I expect). The resurrection of the dead is bodily — not the immortality of a disembodied soul but the transformation of the whole person, soul and body together, into the glorified state. "The life of the world to come" — the eschatological fulfillment of everything creation has been moving toward: the new heaven and new earth (Revelation 21:1), the face-to-face vision of God (visio beatifica), the final and permanent dwelling of God with his people. The creed ends where history ends — and on a note of hope.

The Hail, Holy Queen (Salve Regina)

Hail, holy Queen, Mother of mercy, our life, our sweetness, and our hope. To thee do we cry, poor banished children of Eve; to thee do we send up our sighs, mourning and weeping in this valley of tears. Turn, then, most gracious advocate, thine eyes of mercy toward us; and after this, our exile, show unto us the blessed fruit of thy womb, Jesus. O clement, O loving, O sweet Virgin Mary. Pray for us, O holy Mother of God, that we may be made worthy of the promises of Christ. Amen.Used: closing prayer of the Rosary, Compline (Night Prayer), Marian feasts from Trinity Sunday to Advent

One of the four great Marian antiphons — prayed at the close of the Rosary and at Compline (Night Prayer) during the summer and fall seasons. Attributed to Hermann of Reichenau (11th century). Its emotional register is striking: this is a prayer of exile and longing, addressed to Mary as "our life, our sweetness, and our hope" — language so intimate it discomforts Protestants and moves Catholics to tears. The "valley of tears" motif captures the Catholic theology of earthly life as pilgrimage — we are not home yet, and we need a mother's help to get there. The final invocation — "O clement, O loving, O sweet Virgin Mary" — is among the most tender lines in all of Christian prayer.

How to Pray the Rosary

The Rosary is one of the most common Catholic devotions and one of the most frequently misunderstood. It is not mindless repetition — it is a form of meditative prayer in which the repeated words of the Hail Mary create a background of Scripture (Gabriel's greeting + Elizabeth's greeting + the Church's petition) while the mind meditates on scenes from the lives of Jesus and Mary called the Mysteries. Think of it as praying with both hemispheres simultaneously: the verbal prayer grounds the body while the meditation engages the imagination and intellect.

What You Need

A set of rosary beads: 5 groups of 10 beads (called decades) connected by single beads, with a crucifix pendant and a short section of 3 beads. Available at any Catholic bookstore, many parishes, and online for $2–$200. The beads are not magical — they are a tactile counting device that frees you from counting on your fingers so your mind can focus on the meditation.

The Sequence

1. Hold the crucifix — pray the Apostles' Creed.

2. First large bead — pray the Our Father.

3. Three small beads — pray three Hail Marys (traditionally for faith, hope, and charity).

4. Next large bead — pray the Glory Be and announce the First Mystery. Then pray the Our Father.

5. Ten small beads (a decade) — pray ten Hail Marys while meditating on the announced Mystery.

6. Next large bead — pray the Glory Be and the Fatima Prayer ("O my Jesus, forgive us our sins..."), then announce the next Mystery and pray the Our Father.

7. Repeat steps 5–6 for the remaining four decades.

8. At the end, pray the Hail Holy Queen (see Appendix B).

The Twenty Mysteries (Four Sets of Five)

Joyful Mysteries Monday & Saturday

  1. The Annunciation — Luke 1:26–38. Gabriel appears to Mary. Meditate on: humility and openness to God's will.
  2. The Visitation — Luke 1:39–56. Mary visits Elizabeth; the Magnificat. Meditate on: charity and service.
  3. The Nativity — Luke 2:1–20. Jesus is born in Bethlehem. Meditate on: poverty and detachment from the world.
  4. The Presentation — Luke 2:22–38. Jesus is presented in the Temple. Meditate on: obedience to God's law.
  5. Finding Jesus in the Temple — Luke 2:41–52. The twelve-year-old Jesus found among the teachers. Meditate on: the search for God.

Luminous Mysteries Thursday (added by John Paul II, 2002)

  1. The Baptism in the Jordan — Matthew 3:13–17. The Father speaks; the Spirit descends. Meditate on: fidelity to baptismal promises.
  2. The Wedding at Cana — John 2:1–11. Mary's intercession; the first sign. Meditate on: Mary's intercession and trust in Christ.
  3. The Proclamation of the Kingdom — Mark 1:15. Repentance, conversion, mercy for sinners. Meditate on: call to holiness.
  4. The Transfiguration — Matthew 17:1–8. The glory of Christ revealed. Meditate on: desire for God.
  5. The Institution of the Eucharist — Matthew 26:26–29. The Last Supper. Meditate on: the Real Presence and Eucharistic adoration.

Sorrowful Mysteries Tuesday & Friday

  1. The Agony in the Garden — Matthew 26:36–46. "Not my will but yours be done." Meditate on: contrition and conformity to God's will.
  2. The Scourging at the Pillar — Matthew 27:26. The flogging of Jesus. Meditate on: mortification and purity.
  3. The Crowning with Thorns — Matthew 27:27–31. Mocked as King. Meditate on: courage under contempt.
  4. The Carrying of the Cross — Luke 23:26–32. The road to Calvary. Meditate on: patience in suffering.
  5. The Crucifixion — Luke 23:33–49. "Father, forgive them." Meditate on: final perseverance and love of enemies.

Glorious Mysteries Wednesday & Sunday

  1. The Resurrection — John 20:1–18. The empty tomb; Mary Magdalene. Meditate on: faith and conversion.
  2. The Ascension — Acts 1:9–11. Jesus taken up to heaven. Meditate on: hope in eternal life.
  3. The Descent of the Holy Spirit — Acts 2:1–11. Pentecost. Meditate on: the gifts of the Holy Spirit.
  4. The Assumption of Mary — (tradition; cf. Revelation 12). Mary taken to heaven. Meditate on: the grace of a holy death.
  5. The Coronation of Mary — (tradition; cf. Revelation 12:1). Mary crowned Queen of Heaven. Meditate on: perseverance and final glory.
The Fatima Prayer (prayed after each Glory Be)
O my Jesus, forgive us our sins, save us from the fires of hell, lead all souls to heaven, especially those most in need of your mercy. Amen.Given by Our Lady at Fatima, 1917, to be added after the Glory Be at the end of each decade
Practical Tips

Beginners: pray one decade (one Our Father + ten Hail Marys) at a time rather than the full five-decade rosary. A full rosary covers all five mysteries of one set and takes about 20 minutes at a moderate pace. You can pray it while driving, walking, or doing repetitive tasks. Many people find the Rosary most productive when they close their eyes, breathe slowly, and let the words become a rhythm while the imagination rests quietly on the mystery. You are not trying to feel something intense — you are creating a habitual space of prayer in which God can work.

The Litany of the Saints

Lord, have mercy. Christ, have mercy. Lord, have mercy. Holy Mary, Mother of God — pray for us. Saint Michael — pray for us. Saint Joseph — pray for us. Saint Peter and Saint Paul — pray for us. Saint Andrew — pray for us. [continuing through dozens of saints by name]… All holy men and women, Saints of God — pray for us.Used: the Easter Vigil, ordinations, religious professions, canonizations, in times of grave crisis

The most solemn intercessory prayer in the Catholic repertoire — sung at the most important liturgical moments: the Easter Vigil (while catechumens lie prostrate before baptism), priestly ordinations (while the candidate lies face-down on the floor of the cathedral), religious professions, and papal funerals. The congregation calls on saints by name — Mary, the archangels, the apostles, the early martyrs, the great doctors and founders — and after each name the assembly responds "pray for us." The cumulative effect is overwhelming: you are praying surrounded by two thousand years of witnesses, the entire communion of saints invoked by name and asked to intercede. The practice is rooted in Revelation 5:8, where the prayers of the saints rise like incense before God's throne.

Prayers of Repentance and Forgiveness

The Act of Contrition

O my God, I am heartily sorry for having offended thee, and I detest all my sins because I dread the loss of heaven and the pains of hell, but most of all because they offend thee, my God, who art all good and deserving of all my love. I firmly resolve, with the help of thy grace, to confess my sins, to do penance, and to amend my life. Amen.Used: during the sacrament of Reconciliation (Confession), personal examination of conscience, before sleep

The prayer prayed in the confessional after confessing sins and before receiving absolution. Multiple versions exist; the one above is the traditional English form. The theology is layered: "imperfect contrition" (sorrow motivated by fear of punishment) is valid but inferior to "perfect contrition" (sorrow motivated by love of God — "most of all because they offend thee, my God"). The prayer also includes a firm purpose of amendment — contrition without the intention to change is not genuine repentance. Children memorize this prayer in preparation for First Confession.

The Confiteor

I confess to almighty God, and to you, my brothers and sisters, that I have greatly sinned, in my thoughts and in my words, in what I have done and in what I have failed to do — [striking the breast three times] — through my fault, through my fault, through my most grievous fault. Therefore I ask blessed Mary ever-Virgin, all the Angels and Saints, and you, my brothers and sisters, to pray for me to the Lord our God.Used: at the beginning of Mass (Penitential Act)

Prayed communally at every Mass during the Penitential Act near the beginning. The breast-striking (mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima culpa in Latin) is a physical act of self-accusation — not performative guilt but honest admission of sinfulness before entering the sacred liturgy. The phrase mea culpa has entered common English usage ("it's my mea culpa") because of how deeply embedded this prayer is in Western culture.

Prayers for Protection and Spiritual Combat

Prayer to St. Michael the Archangel

Saint Michael the Archangel, defend us in battle. Be our protection against the wickedness and snares of the devil. May God rebuke him, we humbly pray, and do thou, O Prince of the heavenly hosts, by the power of God, cast into hell Satan, and all the evil spirits, who prowl about the world seeking the ruin of souls. Amen.Used: after Mass (formerly required, now optional but widely restored), personal prayer for protection, spiritual warfare

Composed by Leo XIII in 1886, reportedly after a vision of spiritual combat. Required after every Low Mass until 1964 when it was removed in the post-Vatican II liturgical reform. Many parishes have restored it voluntarily. It is one of the most explicit spiritual warfare prayers in common Catholic use and connects directly to the angelology and demonology discussed in Week 3. Michael is the archangel who defeats Satan in Revelation 12:7–9 and who appears as Israel's defender in Daniel 10:13 and 12:1.

Guardian Angel Prayer

Angel of God, my guardian dear, to whom God's love commits me here, ever this day be at my side, to light and guard, to rule and guide. Amen.Used: morning prayer, children's bedtime prayer, before travel, moments of danger

One of the first prayers Catholic children learn. Reflects the doctrine that each person has a specific guardian angel assigned by God for their protection (Catechism §336, based on Matthew 18:10). The feast of the Guardian Angels is October 2.

Prayers for the Dying and the Dead

Eternal Rest (Requiem Prayer)

Eternal rest grant unto them, O Lord, and let perpetual light shine upon them. May they rest in peace. Amen.Used: funerals, All Souls' Day (November 2), Masses for the dead, when hearing of a death, visiting cemeteries

The fundamental Catholic prayer for the dead. Brief, ancient, and prayed billions of times across history. It presupposes the doctrine of purgatory — the dead may not yet be fully at rest and benefit from the prayers of the living. "Perpetual light" echoes the heavenly vision of Revelation. November is traditionally the month of the Holy Souls, when Catholics intensify prayer for the dead. The prayer is recited at every Catholic funeral and whenever a Catholic hears news of a death — a reflexive response as instinctive as the Sign of the Cross.

The Commendation of the Dying

Go forth, Christian soul, from this world, in the name of God the almighty Father who created you, in the name of Jesus Christ, Son of the living God, who suffered for you, in the name of the Holy Spirit who was poured out upon you. Go forth, faithful Christian…Used: at the deathbed, administered by a priest or any Catholic present

Prayed as a person is dying — the Church's farewell to one of its members. The full prayer is extensive and beautiful, commending the soul to God, to the angels, to the saints, and to "the glorious and ever Virgin Mary, Mother of God." It is part of the Commendatio Animae (Commendation of the Soul), one of the oldest rites in Christianity. Any Catholic can pray it — you do not need a priest, though a priest's presence for the Anointing of the Sick and final Communion (Viaticum, meaning "food for the journey") is strongly desired.

Prayers for Guidance and Strength

Prayer to the Holy Spirit (Veni Creator Spiritus)

Come, Holy Spirit, fill the hearts of your faithful, and enkindle in them the fire of your love. Send forth your Spirit and they shall be created, and you shall renew the face of the earth.Used: before important decisions, at the opening of councils and synods, Confirmation, ordinations, Pentecost

The invocation of the Holy Spirit. Used at the beginning of every ecumenical council, every conclave that elects a pope, every ordination, and every Confirmation. When a Catholic faces a major decision — a career change, a marriage, a vocational discernment — this is the prayer to begin with. The full hymn Veni Creator Spiritus, attributed to Rabanus Maurus (9th century), is one of the most ancient and solemn hymns in Christianity, sung at papal coronations and cathedral dedications.

The Memorare

Remember, O most gracious Virgin Mary, that never was it known that anyone who fled to thy protection, implored thy help, or sought thy intercession was left unaided. Inspired with this confidence, I fly unto thee, O Virgin of virgins, my Mother. To thee do I come, before thee I stand, sinful and sorrowful. O Mother of the Word Incarnate, despise not my petitions, but in thy mercy hear and answer me. Amen.Used: when desperate, when all other resources have been exhausted, personal crisis, examinations, job interviews, before surgery

Attributed to St. Bernard of Clairvaux (12th century), though the attribution is uncertain. The Memorare is the Catholic "emergency prayer" — prayed when you are out of options and need help NOW. Its logic is bold: it claims that no one who has ever asked Mary for help has been refused. Mother Teresa prayed it constantly and reportedly called it her "flying novena" (praying nine Memorares in succession for urgent intentions).

The Serenity Prayer

God, grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, the courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference.Used: 12-step programs, recovery ministry, daily prayer for balance

Not originally Catholic — attributed to the Protestant theologian Reinhold Niebuhr (c. 1932–33) — but widely adopted in Catholic contexts, especially through Alcoholics Anonymous and recovery ministries. Included here because of its deep resonance with the Catholic intellectual tradition: acceptance (Ignatian indifference), courage (the cardinal virtue of fortitude), and wisdom (the Thomistic tradition of practical reason). Many Catholic retreat centers and parish prayer groups use it daily.

Prayers of Praise and Devotion

The Magnificat (Mary's Song)

My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior, for he has looked with favor on the lowliness of his servant… He has brought down the powerful from their thrones, and lifted up the lowly; he has filled the hungry with good things, and sent the rich away empty.Luke 1:46–55. Used: Vespers (Evening Prayer) every day, Marian feasts, the Visitation

Mary's response to Elizabeth's greeting at the Visitation — prayed every day at Vespers (Evening Prayer) throughout the world. It is simultaneously a personal hymn of thanksgiving and one of the most radical social-justice texts in the Bible: God "has brought down the powerful from their thrones and lifted up the lowly; he has filled the hungry with good things and sent the rich away empty." Liberation theologians have made the Magnificat a centerpiece of their theology. Leo XIV's encyclical Magnifica Humanitas draws its title and its hope from this canticle. The Magnificat is also set to music more frequently than almost any other biblical text — settings by Bach, Vivaldi, Pärt, and hundreds of others.

The Anima Christi

Soul of Christ, sanctify me. Body of Christ, save me. Blood of Christ, inebriate me. Water from the side of Christ, wash me. Passion of Christ, strengthen me. O good Jesus, hear me. Within thy wounds hide me. Suffer me not to be separated from thee. From the malignant enemy defend me. In the hour of my death call me, and bid me come unto thee, that with thy saints I may praise thee for ever and ever. Amen.Used: after receiving Communion, personal Eucharistic devotion, Ignatian spirituality

A medieval prayer (14th century) that became central to Ignatian spirituality — St. Ignatius of Loyola placed it at the beginning of his Spiritual Exercises. It is typically prayed after receiving Communion, in the intimate moments of thanksgiving when the believer has just consumed what they believe is the body and blood of Christ. Its language is startlingly physical: "inebriate me," "within thy wounds hide me" — this is not abstract theology but the language of union, desire, and shelter.

Prayers for Specific Life Situations

For Before a Journey / Travel

Lord God, you led Abraham from his homeland and kept him safe through all his travels. Be our companion on this journey. Protect us from danger and bring us safely to our destination, through Christ our Lord. Amen.The patron saint of travelers is St. Christopher (popular devotion, though his feast was removed from the universal calendar in 1969)

For a Sick Person

Lord Jesus Christ, you are the true physician of body and soul. Mercifully visit and heal your servant [Name], granting them strength in body and spirit. Through the intercession of the Blessed Virgin Mary and all the saints, restore them to health if it be your holy will. Amen.The patron saint of the sick is St. Camillus de Lellis; for cancer, St. Peregrine Laziosi; for eye disease, St. Lucy

For a Student Before Exams

Come, Holy Spirit, Divine Creator, true source of light and fountain of wisdom. Enlighten my understanding and grant me a keen intellect, a retentive memory, and the ability to grasp things correctly. Grant me the talent of being exact in my explanations, and the ability to express myself fully and clearly. Guide the beginning of my work, direct its progress, and bring it to successful completion. Through Christ our Lord. Amen.Attributed to St. Thomas Aquinas, patron saint of students and universities

For a Marriage / Spouse

Lord God, you who joined together the first man and woman in a covenant of love, bless our marriage. Help us to love as you love — patiently, generously, without condition. When we fail, grant us the grace of forgiveness. When we are weak, grant us your strength. Keep us faithful to each other and to you, until death completes what your love began. Amen.Patron saints of marriage: the Holy Family; also St. Joseph and the Blessed Mother

For a Deceased Loved One

O God, whose property is always to have mercy and to spare, we humbly beseech thee on behalf of the soul of thy servant [Name], whom thou hast commanded to depart from this world, that thou wouldst not deliver them into the hands of the enemy, but command that they be received by the holy angels, and conducted into paradise. Through Christ our Lord. Amen.Used immediately after a death, during the funeral vigil, during November (the month of the Holy Souls)

The Jesus Prayer

Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner.Used: continuous repetitive prayer (hesychasm), meditation, centering prayer, moments of anxiety

Originally from the Eastern Christian tradition (the Desert Fathers and the Philokalia), the Jesus Prayer is the Christian equivalent of a mantra — repeated continuously, eventually synchronized with breathing, until it becomes as natural as the heartbeat. The anonymous Russian classic The Way of a Pilgrim describes a man who prays it thousands of times daily until it prays itself within him. Though Eastern in origin, it has been adopted across Catholic contemplative communities and is the simplest, most accessible entry point into contemplative prayer. It requires no instruction, no equipment, no special posture — only the willingness to keep repeating the name of Jesus with attention and sincerity.

Novenas: Nine Days of Sustained Prayer

A novena is a prayer prayed for nine consecutive days for a specific intention. The practice is rooted in Acts 1:14: the apostles prayed together for nine days between the Ascension and Pentecost. The logic is persistence: Jesus told the parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1–8) to teach "that they ought always to pray and not lose heart." The novena enacts that persistence structurally. Click any novena below to see the full prayer text and instructions.

✝

Novena to the Holy Spirit

9 Days

When to pray it: This is the oldest novena in Christian history and the origin of the nine-day pattern itself — Mary and the apostles spent nine days in prayer between the Ascension and Pentecost, waiting for the Holy Spirit to come (Acts 1:14). Pray it when you want a deeper experience of the Spirit's presence — for spiritual gifts, for discernment, for a felt sense of God's nearness, or simply to ask the Spirit to fill your heart with joy, one of his Fruits (Galatians 5:22). It is traditionally prayed in the nine days leading up to Pentecost Sunday, but may be prayed at any time of year for any of these intentions.

How to pray it: Pray the prayer below once daily for nine consecutive days. Cardinal Mercier, who popularized this particular text, called it "the secret of sanctity and happiness" and recommended spending about five minutes with it each day — not because the words themselves take five minutes, but because the silence afterward is where the prayer does its real work. After saying the words, sit quietly for a minute or two rather than moving on immediately; let them settle. Then add your own words in your own voice — ask specifically for what you need. The formal prayer opens the door; what you say afterward is just as much a part of the novena as the text itself.

O Holy Spirit, beloved of my soul, I adore You. Enlighten me, guide me, strengthen me, console me. Tell me what I should do; give me Your orders. I promise to submit myself to all that You desire of me and to accept all that You permit to happen to me. Let me only know Your Will. Amen.

Cardinal Mercier's prayer: Désiré-Joseph Mercier (1851–1926), Archbishop of Mechelen-Brussels and one of the most respected Catholic intellectuals of his era, gave this exact text to a young person seeking spiritual guidance, with the instruction to pray it every day for five minutes, closing the eyes and shutting out worldly thoughts. He promised that whoever did this faithfully for the rest of their life would experience a clear conscience, peace in trial, and a deeper sense of God's presence — though, he added, the full fruit might not be felt immediately. Consistency across all nine days matters more than feeling something dramatic on any given day; the dryness of the middle days is normal and does not mean nothing is happening.

✝

The Pentecost Novena (Traditional Nine-Day Form, with the Seven Gifts)

Ascension – Pentecost

When to pray it: This is the oldest and most "official" form of the Novena to the Holy Spirit — older and more structured than the popular Cardinal Mercier prayer above. Pope Leo XIII formally re-established and urged this practice in every parish church in his 1897 encyclical Divinum Illud Munus. Traditionally prayed in the nine days between the Solemnity of the Ascension (the Thursday or Sunday closing the Easter season) and Pentecost Sunday itself, re-enacting the nine days Mary and the apostles spent waiting in prayer in the Upper Room (Acts 1:14). Unlike the Mercier prayer, this novena has a distinct focus each day, meditating in turn on each of the Seven Gifts of the Holy Spirit named in Isaiah 11:2–3.

How to pray it: Each day begins with the Sign of the Cross and the common opening prayer below, followed by that day's specific meditation, then closes with the Glory Be and the traditional Pentecost prayer. Pray once daily for the nine days. If you miss a day, simply continue from where you left off — Catholic devotional practice does not treat a missed day as invalidating the novena; the intention behind the prayer matters more than rigid sequence.

Opening prayer (said every day before the daily meditation):

Almighty and eternal God, who hast vouchsafed to regenerate us by water and the Holy Spirit, and hast given us forgiveness of all our sins, vouchsafe to send forth from heaven upon us thy sevenfold Spirit: the Spirit of Wisdom and Understanding, the Spirit of Counsel and Fortitude, the Spirit of Knowledge and Piety, and fill us with the Spirit of Holy Fear. Amen.

The Nine Daily Meditations

Day 1 — The Holy Spirit Himself: Come, Holy Spirit, and fill the hearts of your faithful. As you descended visibly upon the apostles in tongues of fire, descend invisibly into my soul, that I may know you, love you, and serve you as the Third Person of the Blessed Trinity, fully God, fully present, and fully alive within me from the day of my baptism.

Day 2 — The Gift of Wisdom: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Wisdom, that I may see and judge all things — my work, my relationships, my sufferings, my joys — from the perspective of eternity, valuing them not as the world values them but as God values them.

Day 3 — The Gift of Understanding: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Understanding, that I may grasp, as far as a human mind is able, the truths of the faith I have been taught — not merely to recite them but to penetrate their meaning and let them transform how I see God, myself, and the world.

Day 4 — The Gift of Counsel: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Counsel, that in the many decisions of daily life — large and small — I may readily discern what God is asking of me and choose it without hesitation or fear.

Day 5 — The Gift of Fortitude: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Fortitude, that I may face the trials, temptations, and sufferings of this life with courage, never abandoning what I know to be right out of fear of difficulty, ridicule, or loss.

Day 6 — The Gift of Knowledge: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Knowledge, that I may see created things rightly — neither despising the world God made nor placing in it the ultimate trust and love that belong to God alone.

Day 7 — The Gift of Piety: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Piety, that I may approach God not with the cold duty of a servant but with the trust and affection of a child approaching a loving Father, and that I may extend that same reverence to all whom God has made.

Day 8 — The Gift of Fear of the Lord: O Divine Spirit, grant me the gift of Holy Fear — not the fear of a slave who dreads punishment, but the reverent awe of a child who dreads nothing so much as offending the God who loves them, and who treasures union with God above every other good.

Day 9 — Thanksgiving for the Fruits of the Spirit: O Holy Spirit, source of every spiritual gift, produce in me your fruits — love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22–23). I thank you for every grace received during this novena, and I ask that these fruits, once begun in me, may continue to ripen for the rest of my life.

Closing prayer (said every day, after the daily meditation):

Come, Holy Spirit, fill the hearts of your faithful and kindle in them the fire of your love.
V. Send forth your Spirit, and they shall be created.
R. And you shall renew the face of the earth.

O God, who by the light of the Holy Spirit didst instruct the hearts of the faithful, grant that by the same Holy Spirit we may be truly wise and ever enjoy his consolations, through Christ our Lord. Amen.

How this differs from Cardinal Mercier's prayer (above): Mercier's prayer is a single short text repeated unchanged for nine days, popularized in the 20th century as a vehicle for sustained daily surrender to the Spirit. This older form structures each day around a distinct gift, tracing back to at least the 19th century in its current written form (it is widely attributed to the Congregation of the Holy Ghost Fathers) and reflecting the oldest novena pattern in the Church — the nine days the apostles and Mary spent in prayer between the Ascension and Pentecost. Both are fully legitimate; many Catholics pray Mercier's prayer daily throughout the year and turn to this fuller, gift-by-gift version specifically in the Ascension-to-Pentecost season.

✝

Novena to the Sacred Heart of Jesus

9 Days

When to pray it: For personal transformation, conversion, healing of relationships, and deepening devotion to Christ's love. Traditionally prayed on nine consecutive First Fridays. Can be prayed at any time.

How to pray it: Pray this prayer once each day for nine consecutive days. Add your personal intention after "I ask you to grant..." You may also add an Our Father, Hail Mary, and Glory Be each day.

O most holy Heart of Jesus, fountain of every blessing, I adore you, I love you, and with lively sorrow for my sins I offer you this poor heart of mine. Make me humble, patient, pure and wholly obedient to your will. Grant me, I beg you, the grace of persevering in doing good until the end of my life. Be my strong and feeble heart's defense, for you are my strength and my refuge and my all. Amen.

The First Friday Devotion: Jesus promised St. Margaret Mary Alacoque that those who receive Communion on nine consecutive First Fridays in a spirit of reparation will receive the grace of final perseverance — not dying without the sacraments. This is one of the most beloved devotional promises in Catholic practice.

✝

Novena to Our Lady of Perpetual Help

9 Days

When to pray it: For any persistent, urgent, or seemingly impossible need. One of the most widely prayed novenas in the world. The icon of Our Lady of Perpetual Help (depicting Mary holding the child Jesus while angels show him the instruments of the Passion) is one of the most reproduced images in Catholic history.

How to pray it: Pray once daily for nine days. State your intention clearly after the opening address. Many parishes hold a weekly Wednesday novena to Our Lady of Perpetual Help — check with your parish.

Mother of Perpetual Help, thou art the dispenser of all the gifts which God grants to us miserable sinners; and for this end He has made thee so powerful, so rich, and so bountiful, in order that thou mayest help us in our misery. Thou art the advocate of the most wretched and abandoned sinners who have recourse to thee. Come then to my assistance, for I recommend myself to thee.

In thy hands I place my eternal salvation and to thee do I entrust my soul. Count me among thy most devoted servants; take me under thy protection, and it is enough for me. For, if thou protect me, I fear nothing; not from my sins, because thou wilt obtain for me the pardon of them; nor from the devils, because thou art more powerful than all hell together; not even from Jesus, my Judge himself, because by one prayer from thee He will be appeased. But one thing I fear, that in the hour of temptation I may through negligence fail to have recourse to thee and thus perish miserably. Obtain for me therefore the pardon of my sins, love for Jesus, final perseverance, and the grace ever to have recourse to thee, O Mother of Perpetual Help. Amen.
✝

Novena to St. Jude — Patron of Impossible Causes

9 Days

When to pray it: When the situation seems genuinely hopeless — a terminal diagnosis, a collapsed relationship, an impossible financial situation, a loved one who has left the faith. St. Jude (the apostle, not Judas Iscariot) was invoked for desperate cases because his letter addresses Christians under severe persecution facing seemingly impossible odds.

How to pray it: Pray once daily for nine days. The traditional practice includes a promise to publicize the prayer if it is answered — you have seen these ads in newspapers ("Thank you, St. Jude"). This tradition is optional but reflects the communal nature of prayer.

Most holy Apostle, St. Jude, faithful servant and friend of Jesus, the Church honors and invokes you universally as the patron of difficult cases, of things almost despaired of. Pray for me; I am so helpless and alone. Please help to bring me visible and speedy assistance. Come to my assistance in this great need that I may receive the consolation and help of heaven in all my necessities, tribulations, and sufferings, particularly — [state your intention] — and that I may praise God with you and all the saints forever. I promise, O blessed St. Jude, to be ever mindful of this great favor, to always honor you as my special and powerful patron, and to gratefully encourage devotion to you. Amen.

Follow each day with an Our Father, a Hail Mary, and a Glory Be. On the ninth day, pray this prayer three times.

✝

Novena to St. Joseph

9 Days

When to pray it: For employment and work, for fathers, for the protection of families, for a holy death. St. Joseph is the patron of a happy death because tradition holds that he died in the arms of both Jesus and Mary — no one could ask for a more peaceful end. March 19 (Feast of St. Joseph) and May 1 (Feast of St. Joseph the Worker) are traditional times to begin this novena.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days. The prayer below may be adapted for specific work-related or family intentions.

O Saint Joseph, whose protection is so great, so strong, so prompt before the throne of God, I place in you all my interests and desires. O Saint Joseph, do assist me by your powerful intercession and obtain for me from your divine Son all spiritual blessings through Jesus Christ, Our Lord; so that having engaged here below your heavenly power, I may offer my thanksgiving and homage to the most loving of Fathers.

O Saint Joseph, I never weary contemplating you and Jesus asleep in your arms. I dare not approach while He reposes near your heart. Press Him in my name and kiss His fine head for me, and ask Him to return the kiss when I draw my dying breath. Saint Joseph, patron of departing souls, pray for me. Amen.

Memorare to St. Joseph: "Remember, O most chaste spouse of the Virgin Mary, that never was it known that anyone who implored your help and sought your intercession was left unaided. Inspired by this confidence, I fly unto you and in the midst of my trials and sorrows, I hasten to you. O foster father of the Redeemer, despise not my petition, but in your mercy, hear and answer me. Amen."

✝

The Divine Mercy Novena

Good Friday – Divine Mercy Sunday

When to pray it: Prayed on the nine days between Good Friday and Divine Mercy Sunday (the Second Sunday of Easter). Given to St. Faustina Kowalska (1905–1938) by Jesus himself in her mystical visions. Each day is dedicated to a different group of souls offered to the Father through the wounds of Christ.

How to pray it: Each day, pray the opening prayer, then the day's specific intention, then the Divine Mercy Chaplet (see the Chaplet section). The nine intentions are: (1) All of humanity, (2) Priests and religious, (3) Devout and faithful souls, (4) Those who do not believe and those who do not yet know Jesus, (5) Those who have separated themselves from the Church (heretics and schismatics), (6) The meek and humble and children, (7) Those who especially venerate and glorify Divine Mercy, (8) The souls in purgatory, (9) Souls who have become lukewarm.

Opening prayer (prayed each day):

Eternal Father, I offer you the Body and Blood, Soul and Divinity of Your dearly beloved Son, Our Lord Jesus Christ, in atonement for our sins and those of the whole world. For the sake of His sorrowful Passion, have mercy on us and on the whole world.

Example — Day 1 (Good Friday): All of Humanity

Today, bring to Me all mankind, especially all sinners, and immerse them in the ocean of My mercy. In this way you will console Me in the bitter grief into which the loss of souls plunges Me.

Most Merciful Jesus, whose very nature it is to have compassion on us and to forgive us, do not look upon our sins but upon our trust and confidence which we place in Your infinite goodness. Receive us all into the abode of Your Most Compassionate Heart, and never let us escape from It. We beg this of You by Your love which unites You to the Father and the Holy Spirit.

Eternal Father, turn Your merciful gaze upon all mankind and especially upon poor sinners, all enfolded in the Most Compassionate Heart of Jesus. For the sake of His sorrowful Passion, show us Your mercy, that we may praise the omnipotence of Your mercy forever and ever. Amen.

After the opening prayer and day's intention, pray the Divine Mercy Chaplet (see the Chaplet section below). The full text of all nine days, with each group's specific prayer, is in St. Faustina's Diary: Divine Mercy in My Soul (Marian Press) — the most complete source for all nine days' prayers.

✝

Miraculous Medal Novena

9 Days

When to pray it: For any urgent need, especially financial hardship, employment difficulties, or a situation that feels genuinely impossible. Tied to the 1830 apparitions of Mary to St. Catherine Labouré in Paris, in which Mary showed Catherine the design of a medal and promised that "all who wear it will receive great graces." One of the most widely distributed and widely prayed Marian devotions in the modern Church — over a billion medals have been struck since 1832.

How to pray it: Pray once daily for nine consecutive days, ideally while wearing or holding a Miraculous Medal if you have one (available at any Catholic gift shop or parish gift counter for a few dollars). State your specific intention silently or aloud before beginning.

O Immaculate Mary, Mother of our Savior, and our Mother, from the depths of our hearts we beg you to obtain for us the petition of this novena, if it should be the holy will of God to grant it (here mention your request). You know, O Mary, how often our souls have been the sanctuary of your Son who hates iniquity. Obtain for us then, a deep hatred of sin and that purity of heart which will attach us to God alone, so that our every thought, word, and deed may tend to His greater glory. Obtain for us also a spirit of prayer and self-denial that we may recover by penance what we have lost by sin, and finally, dear Mother, grant that we may have a fervent love of God and our fellow man, perseverance in our resolutions, and the grace of a holy death. O Mary, conceived without sin, pray for us who have recourse to thee. Amen.

The medal itself bears the inscription Mary gave Catherine: "O Mary, conceived without sin, pray for us who have recourse to thee" — a short prayer you can pray throughout the day in addition to the novena.

✝

Novena to St. Anthony of Padua

9 Days

When to pray it: For lost items, lost faith, and lost causes generally. St. Anthony (1195–1231) is one of the most universally beloved saints in Catholic culture — the popular rhyme "Tony, Tony, look around, something's lost and must be found" has real devotional roots in this novena and the centuries-old practice of asking Anthony's help to find lost objects. Beyond physical objects, he is also invoked for lost faith, lost relationships, and lost direction in life.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days. Many people also pray a shorter, spontaneous version when something is actually lost in the moment — the full novena is for sustained intentions.

St. Anthony, perfect imitator of Jesus, who received from God the special power of restoring lost things, grant that I may find (mention your request) which has been lost. At least restore to me peace and tranquility of mind, the loss of which has afflicted me even more than my material loss. To this favor, I ask another of you: that I may always remain in possession of the true good that is God. Let me rather lose all things than lose God himself. May I never suffer the loss of my greatest treasure, eternal life with Him. Amen.

St. Anthony's feast day is June 13. He is also the patron of the poor, travelers, and those seeking a spouse.

✝

Novena to St. Anne

9 Days

When to pray it: For mothers and grandmothers, for those struggling with infertility, for a difficult pregnancy, or for the grace to raise children in faith. St. Anne, the mother of the Blessed Virgin Mary, is venerated as the patron of mothers, grandmothers, and women trying to conceive — her own conception of Mary, according to tradition, came after many years of infertility and fervent prayer.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days, traditionally leading up to her feast day of July 26 (shared with her husband St. Joachim), though it may be prayed at any time.

Good St. Anne, you were especially favored by God to be the mother of the most holy Virgin Mary, the grandmother of our Savior. By your power with your daughter and grandson, intercede for me in my present need (mention your request). You were once a mother awaiting the fulfillment of God's promise; remember now all mothers who wait, who hope, who struggle to bring forth and raise children in faith. Obtain for me the grace to trust as you trusted, to hope as you hoped, and to love as you loved. St. Anne, mother of her who is our life, our sweetness, and our hope, pray for me and obtain my request, if it be the will of God. Amen.
✝

Novena to St. Michael the Archangel

9 Days

When to pray it: For protection, courage in the face of evil, and strength during periods of spiritual struggle or attack. See Week 6's full Spiritual Warfare section for the complete theology behind this devotion. Particularly recommended during difficult or spiritually contested periods — before major life transitions, during times of temptation, or simply as ongoing protection.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days, traditionally leading up to the Feast of the Archangels on September 29, though appropriate at any time of need.

Saint Michael the Archangel, defend us in battle. Be our protection against the wickedness and snares of the devil. May God rebuke him, we humbly pray; and do thou, O Prince of the heavenly host, by the power of God, cast into hell Satan and all the evil spirits who prowl about the world seeking the ruin of souls. Glorious prince of the heavenly armies, defend us in the battle we must wage against principalities and powers, against the rulers of this world of darkness, against the spirits of evil. Come to the aid of all who are oppressed by the enemy, and protect us from his attacks. Guard our families, our minds, and our hearts, that we may remain steadfast in the love of God. Amen.

For the full text, history, and theology of this prayer, including Pope Leo XIII's 1884 vision that inspired it, see the Spiritual Warfare section of Week 6.

✝

Novena to St. Thérèse of Lisieux (The Little Flower)

9 Days

When to pray it: For trust in God's providence during periods of uncertainty, for confirmation that you are on the right path, or simply to grow in her "Little Way" of trusting confidence. St. Thérèse (1873–1897) is famous for promising, before her early death from tuberculosis, to "spend my heaven doing good upon earth" — and for the tradition that she sends a rose as a sign when a prayer has been heard. Countless Catholics report receiving an actual rose, or an image or mention of one, shortly after praying this novena.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days. Many people specifically ask for a rose as a sign, then watch for one in the days following — not as superstition but as a way of staying attentive to how God answers prayer, sometimes through small and unexpected means.

O Little Thérèse of the Child Jesus, please pick for me a rose from the heavenly gardens and send it to me as a message of love. O Little Flower of Jesus, ask God today to grant the favors I now place with confidence in your hands (mention your request). St. Thérèse, help me to always believe as you did in God's merciful love, so that I might imitate your trust amid darkness and your fidelity amid the ordinary, hidden work of each day. Show me, as you promised, that you will let fall a shower of roses; obtain for me the grace I ask, if it be for the glory of God and the good of my soul. Amen.

St. Thérèse's autobiography, Story of a Soul, is one of the most widely read spiritual works in Catholic history and a profound entry point into her "Little Way" — sanctity through small, faithful acts of love rather than dramatic deeds.

✝

Novena for the Holy Souls in Purgatory

9 Days

When to pray it: To pray for the dead — for souls in purgatory undergoing final purification before entering the fullness of heaven. Traditionally prayed throughout November (the month the Church dedicates to remembering the dead, beginning with All Souls' Day on November 2), but appropriate any time you wish to pray for a specific deceased person, or for the souls "forgotten by all" who have no one left on earth to pray for them.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days. This novena pairs naturally with attending Mass, since the Mass is considered the most powerful prayer for the dead in the Catholic tradition, and with the plenary indulgences the Church makes specifically available for the souls in purgatory during November (see Appendix L for the full theology of indulgences).

Eternal Father, I offer Thee the most Precious Blood of Thy Divine Son, Jesus, in union with the Masses said throughout the world today, for all the Holy Souls in Purgatory, for those in my own home and within my family, and for those whose faith is known to Thee alone. O Lord, grant eternal rest to (name, if praying for a specific person), and to all the faithful departed, and let perpetual light shine upon them. May they, through the mercy of God, rest in peace. Amen.

See Week 3 (Purgatory lecture) for the full theology behind this devotion, and Appendix L for the theology of indulgences applicable to the dead.

✝

Novena to Our Lady, Undoer of Knots

9 Days

When to pray it: For untangling complicated relationships, decisions, or situations that feel hopelessly snarled — a difficult marriage, a strained family relationship, a confusing discernment, a problem with no clear solution. This devotion, based on an 18th-century German painting, was personally promoted for decades by then-Cardinal Jorge Bergoglio of Buenos Aires, who brought a print of the image back from Germany in the 1980s — making it one of the devotions most closely associated with Pope Francis before his election.

How to pray it: Once daily for nine days. Some who pray this novena use an actual piece of string or ribbon, tying a knot for each difficulty in the relationship or situation, and symbolically picturing Mary untying each one as they progress through the prayer.

Virgin Mary, Mother of fair love, Mother who never refuses to come to the aid of a child in need, Mother whose hands never cease to serve your beloved children because they are moved by the divine love and immense mercy that exists in your heart, cast your compassionate eyes upon me and see the snarl of knots that exists in my life. You know very well the desperation, the pain, and the darkness of these knots. Mary, Mother to whom God entrusted the undoing of the knots in the lives of His children, I entrust into your hands the ribbon of my life (mention your request). No one, not even the evil one himself, can take it away from your precious care. In your hands there is no knot that cannot be undone. Powerful Mother, by your grace and intercessory power with your Son and Lord, take into your hands today this knot. I beg you to undo it for the glory of God, once for all, for the good of all those involved. Amen.
✝

The St. Andrew Christmas Novena

Nov 30 – Dec 24

When to pray it: Despite the name "novena" (which technically means nine days), this beloved Advent devotion is prayed from the Feast of St. Andrew the Apostle (November 30) through Christmas Eve — about 25 days, spanning nearly all of Advent. It is one of the oldest and most cherished Catholic prayer traditions, of obscure but probably 19th-century Irish origin, and is addressed not to St. Andrew himself but directly to God, asking him to grant a specific request in honor of the birth of his Son.

How to pray it: Traditionally recited fifteen times each day from November 30 through December 24 — either all fifteen at once or divided throughout the day (for example, five times at each meal). State your intention silently before each recitation, or once at the start of each day's set. The repetition of fifteen is historically connected to the medieval monastic practice of reciting the fifteen Gradual Psalms (Psalms 119–133 in the Vulgate) traditionally associated with Mary's own ascent of the Temple steps as a child, according to the apocryphal Protoevangelium of James. The prayer's opening words — "Hail and blessed be the hour and moment" — deliberately place you, in spirit, at the actual moment of Christ's birth, much as a Christmas icon does visually.

Hail and blessed be the hour and moment in which the Son of God was born of the most pure Virgin Mary, at midnight, in Bethlehem, in the piercing cold. In that hour vouchsafe, I beseech Thee, O my God, to hear my prayer and grant my desires (mention your intention) through the merits of Our Savior Jesus Christ, and of His Blessed Mother. Amen.

St. Andrew's feast day falls on or near the First Sunday of Advent (the new liturgical year always begins on the Sunday closest to November 30) — which is why this particular saint's feast, though he has no obvious connection to Christmas itself, became attached to this Advent devotion. See Week 5 for the full liturgical context of Advent and the start of the Church year.

Ten Prayers God Always Says Yes To

God does not always give us what we ask for in the form we imagine it. But Catholic theology identifies a category of prayer so aligned with God's own will that his answer is always, in some form, yes. These are not magic formulas — they are prayers whose content is inseparable from what God most desires to give. The distinction is between asking God to conform to our wishes versus conforming our wishes to his. The prayers below fall into the second category: you cannot ask them sincerely and be refused, because in asking them you are already receiving.

1. "Lord, have mercy." — The Prayer of Humble Need

Kyrie Eleison. Lord, I have nothing. I am asking for mercy, not on the basis of my deserving it but on the basis of who you are.

This is the prayer of the blind beggar Bartimaeus (Mark 10:46–52), the ten lepers (Luke 17:13), the publican in the Temple (Luke 18:13), and the dying thief on the cross (Luke 23:42). It is also the prayer that opens every Mass. The common thread: these people asked for mercy without making a case for why they deserved it. They appealed to God's character rather than their own credentials. Mercy is what God most desires to give — to refuse it would be to contradict himself. The answer is always yes because mercy IS the answer to the question human existence asks.

Scripture: "A broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise." (Psalm 51:17) · "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." (Romans 10:13)

2. "I am sorry. Forgive me." — The Prayer of Contrition

God, I have done wrong. I know it. I am sorry not just because I am afraid but because I love you and I have wounded that love. Please forgive me.

The Catechism teaches that God's forgiveness is never withheld from the truly contrite (§1452). The condition is not perfection of sorrow but sincerity — even imperfect contrition (sorrow rooted in fear of consequences rather than love of God) is sufficient for the sacrament of Confession to work. The parable of the Prodigal Son makes the theological claim explicit: before the son finishes his speech, the father is already running toward him (Luke 15:20). The answer precedes the completion of the asking. The one thing that can block this prayer is insincerity — you cannot ask for forgiveness while planning to repeat the sin — but genuine contrition is never refused.

Scripture: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness." (1 John 1:9)

3. "I believe; help my unbelief." — The Prayer for Faith

God, I want to believe. I am not sure I fully do. Give me more faith than I can produce on my own.

This is the prayer of the father whose son was possessed (Mark 9:24) and the implicit prayer of every person who walks into RCIA not sure why they are there but knowing they cannot stay away. The desire for faith is itself a movement of grace — you would not be seeking unless God were already drawing you. To ask for faith is to ask for the one thing God most wants to give, because faith is how the relationship begins. The Catechism teaches that even the preparation for faith — the seeking, the questioning, the restlessness — is God's own initiative (§153). You cannot sincerely ask for faith and be denied it, because the asking is already God's answer beginning.

Scripture: "Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you. Everyone who asks receives; the one who seeks finds." (Matthew 7:7–8)

4. "Send me your Holy Spirit." — The Prayer for the Spirit

Father, I cannot do this alone. I don't have the love, the patience, the strength, the wisdom, the courage. Send me your Spirit.

Jesus makes this one explicit: "If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!" (Luke 11:13). This is a direct promise — Jesus is not saying God tends to give the Spirit or usually does. He is saying the Father gives the Holy Spirit to those who ask. The prayer for the Spirit is the prayer that unlocks every other prayer: it is the request for the one who "intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words" (Romans 8:26), who prays in us when we don't know what to pray.

Catechism: §2671 — "The Holy Spirit, whose anointing permeates our whole being, is the interior Master of Christian prayer."

5. "Give me wisdom." — The Prayer for Discernment

God, I don't know what to do. I don't know which path is right, which choice honors you, what I'm missing. Give me wisdom I don't have.

James makes this a formal promise: "If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him." (James 1:5). The key phrase is "without reproach" — God does not shame you for not knowing, does not make you feel foolish for asking, does not withhold wisdom as punishment for past failures. He gives it generously, to all, without condition except the asking. Wisdom — not the answer to every question but the capacity to see clearly and choose rightly — is one of the things God most desires to give his children.

Scripture: "Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path." (Psalm 119:105)

6. "Thy will be done." — The Prayer of Surrender

God, I don't know what I need. I thought I did, but I don't. What you want is better than what I want. Have your way with me.

This is the only prayer whose answer is guaranteed by its own content. To pray "thy will be done" is to ask for something already happening — God's will is being done. What the prayer does is align the pray-er's will with what is already occurring, which is itself a transformation. Jesus prayed this in Gethsemane (Luke 22:42) when his human will recoiled from what was coming. He did not pretend the recoil wasn't there — "remove this cup from me" is honest. But he surrendered to the Father's will, and from that surrender came the Resurrection. The prayer of surrender is the prayer that turns suffering into redemption. It cannot be refused because it is not asking for a thing but for a transformation — and God's answer is the transformation itself.

Scripture: "We know that for those who love God all things work together for good." (Romans 8:28)

7. "Give me the grace to resist this." — The Prayer in Temptation

God, I am being pulled toward something I know is wrong. I can feel it. I am not strong enough on my own. Help me.

Paul makes a theological promise here that reads almost like a contract: "God is faithful, and he will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but with the temptation he will also provide the way of escape, that you may be able to endure it." (1 Corinthians 10:13). The promise is not that you will not be tempted — you will. It is that God places a ceiling on temptation proportionate to your capacity with his grace, and provides a way through. The prayer for grace to resist temptation is always answered because God has already, in some sense, pre-answered it by limiting the temptation before it arrives.

Catechism: §2846 — "The Holy Spirit does not allow us to be tempted beyond our strength."

8. "I worship you." — The Prayer of Pure Praise

God, I am not asking for anything right now. I am just here. I am aware of you. You are God and I am not. That is enough. I worship you.

God seeks worshipers: "the Father seeks such people to worship him" (John 4:23). Praise is not something God tolerates — it is something he desires, not because he needs our praise to feel good about himself but because worship is the posture that correctly orients the human person. We were made for this. The Catechism calls praise "the form of prayer which most directly recognizes that God is God" (§2639). A prayer of pure praise — with no petition, no negotiation, no agenda — is always received. It cannot be refused because it is simply the creature acknowledging what is true.

Scripture: "Let everything that has breath praise the Lord." (Psalm 150:6)

9. "Take care of them." — The Prayer of Intercession

God, I am bringing someone to you. I can't fix this for them. I can't make them believe, or heal them, or undo what happened. But I am placing them before you and asking you to act where I cannot.

Intercessory prayer is never wasted. Paul urges "supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings for all people" (1 Timothy 2:1) because God wills "all people to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth" (1 Timothy 2:4). When you pray for someone, you are aligning yourself with what God already wants for them. The prayer may not produce the specific outcome you're imagining — a person's conversion cannot be forced even by God, who respects human freedom. But the prayer is always received, always acted upon, and always does something in the spiritual order that you cannot see. No prayer for another person's good is ever wasted or ignored.

Scripture: "The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working." (James 5:16)

10. "I want to want you." — The Prayer of Longing

God, I do not feel close to you. I am not sure I love you the way I should. I am not even sure I want to pray. But I want to want to. I am here because something in me will not let me stay away.

This is the most important prayer on this list for someone in RCIA — and the one most people don't know they're allowed to pray. You do not have to arrive at faith fully formed. You do not have to love God before you ask God to make you love him. Augustine's "our heart is restless until it rests in thee" describes the experience: the restlessness itself is already God's presence as the one for whom we were made. The Catechism teaches that "the desire for God is written in the human heart" (§27). If you can pray "I want to want you" with any sincerity at all — even tentatively, even skeptically — that desire is God in you, drawing you toward himself. The answer is always yes because the longing is already God's yes, spoken first.

Scripture: "As a deer longs for streams of water, so my soul longs for you, O God." (Psalm 42:1)

✦
A note on unanswered prayer: None of this means God gives you everything you ask for. The Catechism addresses the question directly (§2735–2741): sometimes we ask for the wrong things, sometimes God gives something better than what we asked for, sometimes the timing is not ours to determine, and sometimes suffering itself is the answer — the thing God is working through rather than removing. What the ten prayers above have in common is that they ask for things identical with what God most wants to give: mercy, forgiveness, faith, the Spirit, wisdom, alignment with his will, grace, worship, the good of others, and the longing that leads home. These cannot be refused because they are not negotiating with God — they are opening to what God is already doing.
Appendix C · Practical Reference

Your First Mass: A Step-by-Step Guide

What to expect, what to do, and what to say — for anyone attending a Catholic Mass for the first time or returning after a long absence.

This guide covers the standard Sunday Mass (Novus Ordo, the "ordinary form"). The Traditional Latin Mass follows a different structure. Times, music, and local customs vary by parish — this covers the universal elements.

Before You Arrive

Dress: There is no strict dress code, but "Sunday best" is the general expectation — business casual at minimum. Some parishes are more relaxed; some more formal. When in doubt, err toward respectful. Hats are traditionally removed by men inside the church (women may wear chapel veils but are not required to).

Fasting: Catholics receiving Communion are expected to fast for one hour before (water and medicine are exempt). If you won't be receiving, this doesn't apply to you.

Arriving: Come 5–10 minutes early. When you enter, you'll see a holy water font near the door. Catholics dip their fingers and make the Sign of the Cross. You may do this or simply walk in. Find a pew, genuflect (right knee to the ground) toward the tabernacle if you can identify it (look for a gold box or vault, usually with a red sanctuary lamp burning nearby), then sit quietly. If you're unsure about genuflecting, a respectful bow is always appropriate.

The Mass: What Happens When

Introductory Rites (5–10 minutes)

Entrance procession and hymn: Stand. The priest processes in, usually with altar servers, a lector, and sometimes a deacon. Sing if you know the hymn; the words are usually in a hymnal or projected.

Greeting: The priest says: "The Lord be with you." Everyone responds: "And with your spirit." This response comes up several times during Mass — it's the most important one to know.

Penitential Act: The congregation prays the Confiteor together: "I confess to almighty God and to you, my brothers and sisters, that I have greatly sinned…" Strike your breast gently three times at "through my fault, through my fault, through my most grievous fault." Follow along in the missal or worship aid.

Gloria: On most Sundays (not during Advent or Lent), the Gloria is sung or recited: "Glory to God in the highest…" Stand.

Liturgy of the Word (15–20 minutes)

First Reading: Sit. Usually from the Old Testament. A lector reads from the ambo (podium).

Responsorial Psalm: The cantor sings a psalm verse; the congregation sings the response (printed in the worship aid).

Second Reading: Sit. Usually from an epistle (New Testament letter).

Gospel Acclamation: Stand. "Alleluia" is sung (except during Lent). The priest or deacon processes with the Book of the Gospels.

Gospel Reading: Stand. The priest or deacon says: "The Lord be with you." Response: "And with your spirit." Then: "A reading from the holy Gospel according to [Matthew/Mark/Luke/John]."
