On March 6, 2018, the Vatican Saints Congregation, with authorization from Pope Francis, issued a decree affirming her recovery was "scientifically inexplicable in the light of present-day knowledge." Francis approved the miracle March 7, 2018; Romero was canonized October 14, 2018. (Sources: Vatican Saints Congregation decree, March 6, 2018; America Magazine, March 12, 2018; AP, October 6, 2018; Archbishop Romero Trust.)

Mother Teresa / Teresa of Calcutta (1910–1997)

Feast: September 5Patron of: the poor, missionaries, Calcutta

Founded the Missionaries of Charity in 1950 with 13 members; at her death the congregation had over 4,000 sisters in 130 countries serving the poorest of the poor. Received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979. Her posthumously published letters revealed fifty years of spiritual darkness — "the absence of God is so great," "I feel nothing" — which her directors identified as mystical participation in the desolation of those she served. Canonized by Francis in 2016.

Verified Miracles (Vatican Medical Board): Beatification (2003) — Monica Besra: In September 1998, Monica Besra, a tribal woman in West Bengal, India, was suffering from a large abdominal tumor producing severe headaches and vomiting. After a Miraculous Medal that had touched Mother Teresa's body was placed on her abdomen, the tumor disappeared overnight. A Vatican medical panel reviewed full medical records, CT scans, and physician testimony, concluding the cure was sudden, complete, and medically inexplicable. Canonization (2016) — Marcilio Haddad Andrino: A Brazilian man suffering from multiple brain abscesses fell into a coma; doctors told his wife he had no chance of survival. Following intense prayer by his wife and parish to Mother Teresa's intercession, Andrino awoke from the coma and brain scans showed the abscesses had completely vanished. The Vatican Medical Board found no scientific explanation for the recovery. Pope Francis approved the miracle decree in December 2015. (Sources: Vatican Congregation for the Causes of Saints; AP, Reuters reporting of Vatican press conferences.)

John Paul II (1920–2005)

Feast: October 22Patron of: World Youth Day, families

Karol Wojtyla. Lost mother, brother, and father before age 21. Survived the Nazi occupation of Poland by working in a quarry. First non-Italian Pope in 455 years. Survived an assassination attempt in 1981 and personally forgave his attacker in prison. Traveled to 129 countries. The Theology of the Body, World Youth Day, the canonization of more saints than all predecessors combined, the historic visit to the Synagogue of Rome, the diplomatic contribution to communism's fall, the Catechism of the Catholic Church (1992), the Jubilee Year 2000 apologies for the Church's historical sins — all in 26 years. The crowd's spontaneous chant at his funeral: "Santo subito" — sainthood now. Canonized by Francis in 2014.

Verified Miracles (Vatican Medical Board): Beatification (2011) — Sister Marie Simon-Pierre: A French nun of the Little Sisters of Catholic Motherhood who was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease. Two months after John Paul II's death (2005), following prayer within her congregation asking for John Paul II's intercession, she awoke one morning with her Parkinson's symptoms completely resolved. She has shown no recurrence. The Vatican medical panel found no scientific explanation for the sudden, complete, and lasting cure. Canonization (2014) — Floribeth Mora Díaz: A Costa Rican woman suffering from a cerebral aneurysm was given days to live. On May 1, 2011 — the day of John Paul II's beatification — after her husband opened the newspaper to a photograph of John Paul II and she prayed for his intercession, she experienced an immediate, complete healing that her doctors described as medically inexplicable. Pope Francis signed the canonization decree in July 2013. (Sources: Vatican press office statements; multiple verified news agency reports.)

Appendix F · Reading List

Book Summaries

Concise critical summaries of eleven books directly relevant to this seminar — covering the Dead Sea Scrolls, intelligent design, marriage, conversion, and the problem of Old Testament ethics. Each summary includes central claim, supporting arguments, key assumptions, counterarguments addressed, critical assessment, connections to the course, notable quotes, and actionable takeaways.

John Bergsma

Central Claim

The Dead Sea Scrolls — discovered at Qumran between 1947 and 1956 — illuminate the Jewish world Jesus inhabited so precisely that they transform our understanding of the Gospels, John the Baptist, the Eucharist, and early Christian community structures. Christianity is not a Hellenistic innovation grafted onto Judaism; it is a Jewish movement explainable from within Second Temple Judaism itself.

Supporting Arguments

John the Baptist and the Essenes: The Qumran community lived in the same Judean desert where John baptized, followed a similar lifestyle of asceticism and ritual washing, quoted the same Isaiah 40:3 passage ("prepare the way of the Lord") as their founding text, and waited for a priestly messiah. Bergsma argues the connections are too specific to be coincidental and that John likely spent time with or was influenced by the community.

The Sacred Meal: The Qumran community held a highly regulated communal meal with bread and wine presided over by a priest — a practice described in the Community Rule (1QS). The Dead Sea Scrolls' messianic texts describe this meal as anticipating the messianic banquet when the Messiah comes. Bergsma argues this provides the Jewish sacramental background against which Jesus's Last Supper and the Eucharist are best understood.

New Covenant Theology: The Qumran community understood itself as the remnant Israel living under the "New Covenant" prophesied by Jeremiah 31. They used the same term Jesus used at the Last Supper ("new covenant in my blood"). This was not Christian vocabulary borrowed by a Jewish sect — it was Jewish vocabulary used by Jesus with full awareness of its sectarian resonance.

Community Structures: The Qumran community's organizational structure (a council of twelve, a presiding officer, communal property sharing, a practice of reproof and expulsion for sin) parallels early Christian community organization in Acts 1-5 in ways that suggest a common inheritance rather than coincidence.

Key Assumptions

The Qumran community produced the Dead Sea Scrolls — the majority scholarly position, though a small minority dispute it. Historical-critical and confessional Catholic methods can be integrated without contradiction. Structural and thematic parallels between Qumran and early Christianity are evidence of influence, not mere coincidence.

Counterarguments Addressed

Bergsma engages the objection that the parallels are overstated and that the differences between Qumran and Christianity (Jesus's table fellowship with sinners vs. Qumran's exclusivity; Jesus's attitude toward the Temple vs. Qumran's; messianic identification) are as significant as the similarities. He acknowledges the differences while arguing the similarities are too specific to dismiss.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Norman Golb, Who Wrote the Dead Sea Scrolls? (1995): Golb's most disruptive argument is that the scrolls were not produced at Qumran at all but were a library from Jerusalem, evacuated before the Roman siege and hidden in the desert. If Golb is right, the Qumran-Christianity connection dissolves entirely — there was no sectarian community at Qumran producing documents, only a collection point for a diverse Jerusalem library. Golb argued that the caves contained too many different handwriting styles and theological perspectives to represent a single sect's output. Mainstream DSS scholarship has largely not accepted Golb's thesis, but it remains a live minority position that any serious student of the field must reckon with.

Lawrence Schiffman, Reclaiming the Dead Sea Scrolls (1994): Schiffman, a Jewish scholar, argues the Qumran community was far closer to Sadducean Judaism than to proto-Christianity or proto-rabbinic Judaism — and that the Christian parallels Bergsma emphasizes are therefore coincidental convergences on shared Jewish practices rather than evidence of direct influence on early Christianity. On the communal meal specifically, Schiffman argues the parallels are less precise than they appear once you account for the wide range of Jewish purification meals that existed in this period. The Qumran meal was a purity meal for a priestly community; the Christian Eucharist is a memorial-sacrifice — structurally and theologically distinct even if both use bread and wine.

The Independence Hypothesis: A significant strand of DSS scholarship holds that both the Qumran community and early Christians drew independently on the same rich stream of Second Temple Jewish apocalypticism, covenant theology, and New Covenant expectation — shared sources that would produce similar-looking practices without any causal relationship between the communities. This position acknowledges all of Bergsma's parallels as real while denying that they demonstrate influence rather than common inheritance.

Critical Assessment

Bergsma is a credentialed Dead Sea Scrolls scholar and this is his strongest scholarly contribution for general readers. The book successfully bridges technical DSS scholarship and Catholic theological interpretation. The most contested claim is the direct causal connection between Qumran practices and early Christian ones — correlation does not automatically establish influence. Some scholars argue both communities drew independently on shared Jewish traditions. The Eucharistic parallel in particular is debated: the Qumran meal resembles a preparatory anticipation more than a sacramental celebration. These are live scholarly debates, not settled questions, and Bergsma is more confident than the evidence strictly warrants.

Connections to the Course

Directly relevant to Week 2 (The Bible as Library, Jewish roots of Catholic worship), Week 4 (Eucharist), Week 5 (Liturgical life), and the appendix on Key Biblical Passages. Pairs naturally with Pitre's Jesus and the Jewish Roots of the Eucharist.

Notable Quote
"If you want to understand what Jesus was doing and saying, you need to understand the world he was born into — and no documents illuminate that world more vividly than the Dead Sea Scrolls."— John Bergsma, Jesus and the Dead Sea Scrolls (2019)
Actionable Takeaways
  • When reading the Gospels, bring Second Temple Jewish sectarian context — not Greco-Roman philosophical categories — as your primary interpretive lens.
  • The Eucharist is not a Greek mystery religion transplanted onto Judaism — it emerges from within Jewish sacrificial and covenant meal theology.
  • The twelve apostles, communal property sharing, and fraternal correction in early Acts are Jewish community practices, not Roman organizational innovations.
Central Claim

Scripture presents a coherent, unified theology of marriage from Genesis through Revelation — a theology of permanent, faithful, fruitful, self-giving union between one man and one woman that finds its ultimate meaning as an image of the relationship between Christ and the Church. The distortions of this design throughout the biblical narrative (polygamy, divorce, adultery) are presented as deviations, not endorsements.

Supporting Arguments

Genesis as Foundation: Genesis 2:24 ("the two shall become one flesh") is the charter text for all biblical marriage theology. Bergsma reads it through the lens of the covenantal structure of the entire Bible — the one-flesh union mirrors the covenant God makes with Israel. The nakedness without shame (Gen 2:25) represents the total self-gift of persons who hold nothing back.

The Narrative of Distortion: Throughout the Old Testament — from Lamech's polygamy (Gen 4) through the patriarchs, David, and Solomon — marriage is repeatedly distorted. Bergsma argues that these narratives are not endorsements of polygamy but demonstrations of its consequences, structured to show what happens when God's original design is abandoned. The suffering in these marriages is the point.

The Prophetic Tradition: Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Malachi use marriage as the primary metaphor for God's covenant with Israel — and unfaithfulness in marriage as the image for Israel's idolatry. This elevates marriage to a theological category, not merely a social contract.

Paul's Ephesians 5 Framework: The husband-wife relationship is explicitly a participation in and image of the Christ-Church relationship. This is not a subordination argument primarily — it is a sacramental argument: human marriage images the divine-human union, which is why it is indissoluble.

Key Assumptions

The Bible's diverse marriage narratives are unified by a single divine design. John Paul II's Theology of the Body is the authentic contemporary development of this biblical vision. The Catechism's treatment of matrimony (§§1601–1666) correctly reads the biblical data.

Counterarguments Addressed

Bergsma addresses the apparent approval of polygamy and the divorce provisions in Mosaic law (Deuteronomy 24) directly, engaging Jesus's own response in Matthew 19:8 ("Moses permitted it because of the hardness of your hearts — from the beginning it was not so") as the key. He also addresses the "exception clauses" in Matthew 5:32 and 19:9 regarding porneia.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

William J. Webb, Slaves, Women and Homosexuals (2001) — Redemptive-Movement Hermeneutics: Webb argues that Scripture should be read not as a fixed final ethic but as a trajectory moving toward an ultimate ethic that the biblical authors themselves were pointing beyond. The trajectory of Scripture on marriage, women, and slavery moves consistently in the direction of greater dignity and equality — but the final destination of that trajectory may lie beyond what any individual biblical text explicitly states. Applied to marriage, this produces a different conclusion than Bergsma's: the divorce provisions and the patriarchal marriage structures in the OT are not merely concessions to sinfulness but genuine moral progress over the surrounding culture, and the trajectory they set in motion is not complete in the NT. Webb would not dispute that indissolubility is the direction Scripture points, but his method is more permissive of contemporary exceptions where the redemptive trajectory seems to require them.

David Instone-Brewer, Divorce and Remarriage in the Bible (2002): Instone-Brewer argues from the ancient Jewish context that Jesus's teaching on divorce — which Bergsma reads as establishing near-absolute indissolubility — was actually addressing a specific 1st-century debate between the schools of Hillel (who permitted divorce for almost any reason) and Shammai (who permitted it only for adultery). Jesus was siding with Shammai, not establishing an entirely new and stricter standard. Instone-Brewer concludes that the Jewish marriage contract (the ketubah) assumed divorce was permissible for serious violation of marital duties (abandonment, neglect, abuse) and that Jesus did not overturn this — he only corrected the Hillelite laxity. This reading, if accepted, significantly loosens the Catholic position on indissolubility and has been influential in some Protestant traditions.

Critical Assessment

Published in September 2024, this is the most recent of Bergsma's popular-level works. It serves primarily as a pastoral and apologetic resource rather than a purely academic study. Its strongest contribution is integrating the full sweep of the biblical narrative into a coherent theology of marriage rather than proof-texting isolated passages. The pastoral use case — for couples in marriage preparation or for Catholics trying to understand indissolubility — is clear. The academic engagement is lighter than his Dead Sea Scrolls work.

Connections to the Course

Directly relevant to Week 4 (Catholic Marriage Process, Theology of the Body), Week 7 (Theology of the Body — JPII). The biblical foundation for what JPII developed philosophically.

Notable Quote
"Throughout both the Old and New Testaments, marriages disfigured God's original design for marriage as a lifelong, faithful, and sacrificial union between one man and one woman — and the narrative shows us the consequences."— Reviewer summary of Bible and Marriage
Actionable Takeaways
  • Read Genesis 1–2 and Ephesians 5 together as a frame for the whole biblical theology of marriage before entering Pre-Cana.
  • The OT's polygamous patriarchs are a cautionary tale, not a permission slip — the narrative structure makes this clear when read attentively.
  • The theological grounding for marriage's indissolubility is not an arbitrary Church rule but the sacramental participation in the Christ-Church covenant, which is itself indissoluble.
Central Claim

The Catholic teachings most frequently criticized as "unbiblical" by Protestants — the papacy, Marian devotion, sacramental Confession, the Real Presence in the Eucharist, the Catholic priesthood — are in fact robustly supported by Scripture. A Protestant pastor who took the Bible seriously found himself, against his initial intentions, becoming Catholic.

Supporting Arguments

The book covers seven doctrines, each with the strongest Protestant objection stated honestly and the Catholic biblical case made in response:

The Papacy: Matthew 16:17–19 ("You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my Church") in its Jewish context. The keys of the kingdom as the Davidic office of prime minister (Isaiah 22:20–22). Peter's unique role in every list of apostles, in Acts, and in Paul's letters.

Mary: Luke 1:41–45 (Elizabeth's greeting parallels the Ark narrative in 2 Samuel 6); Revelation 12 (the woman clothed with the sun); Mary's perpetual virginity in patristic interpretation of Matthew 1:25.

Confession: John 20:22–23 ("Receive the Holy Spirit. Whose sins you forgive are forgiven; whose sins you retain are retained"); James 5:16 ("Confess your sins to one another").

The Eucharist: John 6:53–58 (the Bread of Life discourse, read as literal in context); 1 Corinthians 11:27–29 (eating the bread "unworthily" brings judgment — hard to understand if it's merely symbolic).

The Priesthood: Hebrews 7–10 (Christ as high priest); the sacrificial framework of the Mass; Old Testament typology.

Sola Scriptura (Bible Alone): The canon of Scripture was determined by the Church — Scripture cannot authenticate itself without a prior authority. 2 Timothy 3:16 says Scripture is "God-breathed," not "self-interpreting."

Sola Fide (Faith Alone): James 2:24 ("A person is justified by works and not by faith alone") — the only place "faith alone" appears in Scripture, and it's a denial.

Key Assumptions

Scripture and Catholic Tradition are not in conflict. Patristic consensus is relevant interpretive data. The historical-critical method, when applied carefully, supports rather than undermines Catholic readings.

Counterarguments Addressed

Bergsma was a Protestant pastor for years before his conversion, so he gives Protestant arguments their strongest form before responding. This is not a straw-man apologetics book — the objections are real ones that he personally held.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

James R. White, The Roman Catholic Controversy (1996): White, a Reformed Baptist apologist, represents the most rigorous Protestant scholarly response to exactly the arguments Bergsma makes. On Peter and the papacy: White argues that the Greek text of Matthew 16:18 distinguishes between Petros (Peter's name, masculine) and petra (rock, feminine) — the rock on which the Church is built is Peter's confession of faith, not Peter himself. On sola scriptura: White argues Bergsma misunderstands the doctrine — sola scriptura does not claim Scripture is the only source of knowledge about God but that it is the only infallible rule of faith and practice, which does not require Scripture to authenticate itself without a prior tradition, only that the tradition is always accountable to Scripture. On the Eucharist: White argues John 6 uses the same kind of figurative language ("I am the vine, I am the door") that Jesus employs throughout John's Gospel, and that reading it as literal requires ignoring the literary context.

Keith Mathison, The Shape of Sola Scriptura (2001): Mathison distinguishes between sola scriptura (Scripture as the supreme and final authority, interpreted within the community of the Church through the creeds and ecumenical councils) and solo scriptura (the individual reader interpreting Scripture independently without reference to tradition). He argues Bergsma attacks solo scriptura but that this is a position most serious Reformed theologians also reject — the real debate is whether the Catholic Magisterium has the authority to be the final interpreter, not whether tradition matters. This distinction partially concedes Bergsma's point about canon formation while denying its conclusion about papal authority.

R.C. Sproul on Justification: On Bergsma's sola fide argument (citing James 2:24 as the only NT "faith alone" verse, and it's a denial), Reformed theologians respond that James is addressing a different context than Paul — James's opponents claim faith without works of any kind, while Paul's opponents claim justification through the works of the Mosaic law. Paul and James are not in contradiction because they're answering different questions. The Catholic reading that conflates these contexts misreads both authors.

Critical Assessment

At 144 pages this is deliberately accessible rather than exhaustive — each doctrine could fill a book on its own. It won't satisfy a determined Protestant apologist, but it isn't aimed at them. The target audience is Catholics who can't explain why they believe what they believe, inquirers who've heard the "unbiblical" charge, and Protestants genuinely curious about the Catholic claim. Bergsma's personal conversion story adds credibility that a purely argumentative text wouldn't have. The weakest section is probably the Marian material — the parallels with the Ark narrative are suggestive but require a typological hermeneutic that not everyone will accept on first encounter.

Connections to the Course

Directly relevant throughout — especially Week 1 (authority, papacy), Week 4 (sacraments, Eucharist), the RCIA Companion Guide. The best single book to give someone who asks "Where is that in the Bible?"

Notable Quote
"Over an eighteen-month spiritual journey, Bergsma was stunned again and again by the biblical support he found for even the stickiest teachings of the Catholic Church."— Publisher description
Actionable Takeaways
  • Read this during or before OCIA — it provides the scriptural basis for everything you'll be asked to believe.
  • Keep it as a reference when Protestant family members ask where Catholic teachings come from.
  • The sola scriptura chapter alone is worth the price — it explains why the Catholic appeal to Tradition alongside Scripture is not an abandonment of the Bible but a prior logical necessity.

Stephen C. Meyer

Central Claim

The digital information encoded in DNA — its specified complexity, its four-letter chemical alphabet writing functional instructions — constitutes powerful evidence for intelligent design, because information of this kind demonstrably arises only from intelligence, not from undirected chemistry or chance.

Supporting Arguments

The Information Problem: DNA is genuinely analogous to a computer program — it stores digital information in a four-letter chemical alphabet (A, T, G, C) that specifies the order of amino acids in proteins. This is not metaphor; it is a literal description of how molecular biology works, confirmed by Nobel Prize-winning science since Watson and Crick (1953).

Specified Complexity: Drawing on information theorist William Dembski's work, Meyer argues that functional information has two properties: complexity (not likely by chance) and specification (matches an independently describable functional pattern). Specified complexity is, empirically, only ever observed to arise from intelligence. DNA has both properties in abundance.

Failure of Competing Hypotheses: Meyer systematically evaluates every major naturalistic hypothesis for the origin of life's information: RNA world, self-organization, chemical necessity, and various combinations. Each fails on either probabilistic or chemical grounds. The space of possible amino acid sequences is so vast that random search cannot find the functional ones in any realistic timeframe.

Uniformitarian Reasoning: Meyer uses the same logical inference structure that Charles Lyell and Darwin used — "the present is the key to the past." We know from uniform and repeated experience what produces functional information. That cause is intelligence. Therefore the best explanation for DNA's information is prior intelligence.

Key Assumptions

The DNA-to-code analogy is a genuine identity, not merely a loose metaphor. Probability calculations can meaningfully be applied to chemical events in pre-biotic conditions. Inference to the best explanation (abduction) is a legitimate scientific method.

Counterarguments Addressed

Meyer extensively addresses the RNA world hypothesis (the idea that self-replicating RNA preceded DNA), responding that RNA faces the same information problem as DNA. He engages Fred Hoyle's panspermia hypothesis, Robert Shapiro's metabolist alternative, and self-organization theories (Kauffman). He also addresses the charge that ID is not science — arguing that it uses the same inferential structure as historical sciences like archaeology and forensic science.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Jack Szostak, Nobel Prize laureate (Chemistry, 2009), RNA world research: Szostak's laboratory has produced the most experimentally advanced work on the RNA world hypothesis — the scenario in which self-replicating RNA molecules preceded both DNA and proteins, solving what Meyer calls the chicken-and-egg problem (DNA needs proteins to replicate; proteins need DNA to be specified). Szostak's group has demonstrated that short RNA sequences can catalyze their own replication under plausible prebiotic conditions, that fatty-acid vesicles (proto-cell membranes) can form spontaneously and incorporate RNA, and that replication can occur within them. Szostak argues Meyer fundamentally mischaracterizes the RNA world hypothesis by demanding it solve the full information problem at once — in fact, the hypothesis requires only a much simpler initial replicator that natural selection can then improve. The probability calculations Meyer uses assume a fully specified modern protein, not a simpler ancestral function, which makes his numbers wildly unrepresentative of what origin-of-life science actually proposes.

Kenneth R. Miller, Only a Theory: Evolution and the Battle for America's Soul (2008): Miller, a Catholic cell biologist at Brown University, makes the sharpest scientific critique: Meyer's information argument confuses the origin of information with the origin of specified functional complexity. Shannon information — which is what DNA contains — can increase by natural processes without design. The question is not whether information can arise naturally (it demonstrably can in physical systems) but whether functional information of the kind in DNA requires design. Miller argues Meyer defines "specified complexity" in a way that makes it unfalsifiable — any arrangement that functions is retroactively deemed to require design, which makes the argument a tautology rather than an empirical claim.

Elliott Sober, Evidence and Evolution (2008): Philosopher of science Sober argues that the design inference Meyer relies on — "information only comes from minds, therefore DNA's information came from a mind" — is a fallacious induction. The premise is derived entirely from our experience of human-designed systems. Generalizing from human artifacts to the origin of life commits what Sober calls the "problem of the reference class": we have no experience of universes coming into existence or life originating, so we have no basis for probability calculations about these events. Meyer's inference to the best explanation works only if we already know the range of possible explanations — which, for the origin of life, we do not.

Critical Assessment

Signature in the Cell is the most technically rigorous popular-level treatment of the information argument for ID. Meyer's background is philosophy of science (Cambridge PhD) rather than molecular biology, which some scientists cite as a limitation. The main scientific objections: (1) Meyer's probability calculations assume protein folds must be found by random search, ignoring evolutionary pathways that don't require starting from scratch; (2) the DNA-to-code analogy, while useful, may import misleading assumptions from human-designed codes to chemical processes; (3) ID is not falsifiable and therefore fails as a scientific claim. The strongest version of the rebuttal holds that Meyer has not eliminated all naturalistic possibilities but has only shown that current hypotheses are inadequate — which is a different claim. Worth reading alongside critical responses from origin-of-life scientists such as Jack Szostak.

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Catholicism and Science, Lemaître and complementarity), the Thaller/JRE discussion of science's limits. Provides the biological arm of the cumulative ID argument that Meyer completes in Return of the God Hypothesis.

Notable Quote
"The information in DNA is not just complex, it is specified — it functions to produce specific biological outcomes. And specified complexity is the hallmark of intelligence."— Stephen C. Meyer, Signature in the Cell (2009)
Actionable Takeaways
  • Understand the distinction between complexity (improbable) and specified complexity (improbable AND functional) — this is the core of the information argument.
  • The origin of life is a genuinely unsolved scientific problem. Neither theists nor atheists should pretend otherwise.
  • Read alongside Szostak's popular-level work on RNA world for the other side of the scientific debate.
Central Claim

The Cambrian explosion — the geologically sudden appearance of most major animal body plans approximately 530 million years ago — cannot be explained by Neo-Darwinian mechanisms (random mutation plus natural selection) and constitutes positive evidence for intelligent design. Darwin himself identified this as the most serious challenge to his theory; it remains unresolved.

Supporting Arguments

The Phenomenon: In a geologically brief window (5–10 million years by most estimates, brief by geological standards), the fossil record shows the sudden appearance of most major animal phyla — fundamentally different body plans — without clear Precambrian ancestors. Darwin predicted the fossil record should show a gradual branching tree; the Cambrian shows a sudden bush.

The Information Problem Extended: Building new body plans requires not just new proteins but new gene regulatory networks — the developmental programs that control how genes are expressed during embryonic development. These networks are extraordinarily sensitive to disruption (small changes cause lethality or sterility), making them essentially impervious to the gradual modification that Darwinism requires.

Top-Down Pattern: The fossil record shows phyla appearing before classes, classes before orders — the reverse of what Darwin predicted. New body plans appear at the top of the taxonomic hierarchy first, then are elaborated below. This is the opposite of the Darwinian expectation of small variations accumulating to produce large ones.

Epigenetics: Recent discoveries in epigenetics (inherited information beyond DNA sequence) compound the information problem — body plan information is not solely in the genome but in the three-dimensional structure of the cell, which adds another layer of specified complexity that gradualist mechanisms cannot plausibly produce.

Key Assumptions

The Cambrian explosion cannot be explained by undiscovered Precambrian ancestors or extended time frames. The gene regulatory network argument is robust. Epigenetic inheritance genuinely compounds rather than dissolves the problem.

Counterarguments Addressed

Meyer engages Charles Marshall's (UC Berkeley paleontologist) detailed response directly — Marshall argues that "new genes" rather than regulatory rewiring explain the explosion. Meyer responds that this concedes the information problem rather than solving it. He addresses the Ediacaran fauna (pre-Cambrian complex organisms) and argues they are not gradual precursors to Cambrian body plans.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Charles R. Marshall, "When Prior Belief Trumps Scholarship," Science 341 (2013): Marshall's formal review in Science — the most prestigious peer-reviewed scientific journal — is the most authoritative mainstream paleontological response to Meyer's book. Marshall's core argument: the Cambrian explosion is not the mystery Meyer presents because Meyer confuses the origin of animal body plans with the origin of the genetic information underlying them. Modern evolutionary developmental biology (evo-devo) shows that the evolution of new body plans does not require the creation of wholly new regulatory networks from scratch — it requires the rewiring of existing ones through relatively small genetic changes that have large developmental effects. The same toolkit genes (Hox genes, Pax genes) used in building a fly's body are used in building a human's; the difference lies in how they are deployed, not in fundamentally different information. Marshall's conclusion: Meyer has written a book for a popular audience using technical-sounding arguments that misrepresent both the fossil record and the current state of evolutionary developmental biology.

Donald Prothero, Evolution: What the Fossils Say and Why It Matters (2007): Prothero, a paleontologist at Caltech, argues that Meyer systematically misrepresents the quality of the pre-Cambrian fossil record. The Ediacaran fauna (560–540 million years ago) represent genuine anatomically complex organisms that precede the Cambrian and show the gradual elaboration Meyer denies. The reason we don't see more Precambrian animal fossils is taphonomic (preservation bias) — soft-bodied organisms rarely fossilize. The apparent suddenness of the Cambrian explosion is partly an artifact of how well Cambrian organisms preserved compared to their predecessors, not evidence of their sudden creation.

Nick Matzke, detailed technical critique (Panda's Thumb blog, 2013): Matzke, who provided scientific expertise in the Kitzmiller v. Dover intelligent design case, published a detailed technical review demonstrating that Meyer miscites scientific literature — in several cases attributing to papers conclusions that are the opposite of what those papers actually argue. Matzke's critique focuses specifically on Meyer's treatment of regulatory gene networks, arguing that Meyer misunderstands the role of developmental constraints and that the "top-down" pattern in the fossil record is consistent with, not contrary to, evolutionary theory.

Critical Assessment

Darwin's Doubt generated the most substantive scientific pushback of Meyer's three books. Marshall's review in Science (2013) represents the mainstream paleontological response: the Cambrian explosion is a genuine anomaly but not one that defeats evolutionary theory, because the relevant mechanisms (regulatory evolution) were not available to Darwin and are now better understood. Meyer's response to Marshall (available on the Discovery Institute website) extends the argument. The honest assessment: the Cambrian explosion is a real and acknowledged puzzle in evolutionary biology, not a manufactured controversy; whether it specifically requires ID or merely incomplete evolutionary theory is genuinely debated by scientists, not just philosophers.

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Catholicism and Science). The second volume in Meyer's ID trilogy, building on Signature in the Cell and setting up Return of the God Hypothesis.

Notable Quote
"The Cambrian explosion — Darwin's doubt — has not been resolved by subsequent fossil discoveries. It has, if anything, become more acute."— Stephen C. Meyer, Darwin's Doubt (2013)
Actionable Takeaways
  • Read Marshall's Science review alongside Meyer's book — this is what rigorous scientific engagement with ID looks like from the mainstream side.
  • The Cambrian explosion is a real phenomenon worth knowing about regardless of where you land on ID — it's a significant feature of the history of life.
  • The distinction between "current evolutionary theory cannot explain X" and "evolution cannot in principle explain X" is crucial — Meyer argues the latter, critics say only the former.
Central Claim

Three major scientific discoveries — cosmological fine-tuning, the digital information in DNA, and the emergence of consciousness — together form a cumulative case that the universe had a beginning, was finely tuned for life by a prior intelligence, and that mind precedes matter rather than arising from it. These three lines of evidence converge on theism as the best explanation.

Supporting Arguments

Cosmological Fine-Tuning: The fundamental constants of physics (gravitational constant, electromagnetic force, the cosmological constant, the ratio of proton to electron mass, and dozens of others) are set to extraordinary precision for life to be possible. Change any by small amounts and the universe produces no stars, no chemistry, no life. The probability of this by chance is vanishingly small. The main alternative — the multiverse — is unobservable, unfalsifiable, and speculative.

The Big Bang and the Beginning: Modern cosmology confirms that the universe had a beginning (the Big Bang, further evidenced by cosmic expansion, the CMB radiation, and the second law of thermodynamics). Whatever begins to exist has a cause. The universe began to exist. Therefore it has a cause — one that transcends space, time, matter, and energy. This is the cosmological argument updated for 21st-century physics.

Biological Information: Extends the Signature in the Cell argument — DNA's information points to prior intelligence at the origin of life.

Consciousness: The hard problem of consciousness (why there is something it is like to be a physical system) resists materialist explanation. Human rational consciousness — including its ability to access abstract mathematical truths — suggests a mind-first rather than matter-first metaphysics.

Key Assumptions

The multiverse hypothesis does not dissolve the fine-tuning argument. Inference to the best explanation is legitimate in cosmology. The three arguments are genuinely independent (not three versions of the same argument) and therefore compound each other's evidential weight.

Counterarguments Addressed

The multiverse gets the most sustained treatment — Meyer argues it is scientifically untestable, philosophically motivated, and actually compounds rather than eliminates the design inference (a finely tuned multiverse-generating mechanism still requires explanation). He engages Richard Dawkins's "who designed the designer?" objection, Stephen Hawking's no-boundary proposal, and Laurence Krauss's "universe from nothing" argument.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Victor J. Stenger, The Fallacy of Fine-Tuning (2011): Stenger, a particle physicist, makes the most technically detailed case against Meyer's fine-tuning argument. His core claim: the fine-tuning argument depends on varying one constant at a time while holding all others fixed, but the constants of physics are not independent variables — they are related through deeper physical principles we don't yet fully understand. When Stenger varies multiple constants simultaneously in computer simulations, he finds that a significant range of possible universes (up to a third, in some analyses) would be life-permitting, because different combinations of constants can produce stable chemistry. If life-permitting universes are not rare but common across the parameter space, the fine-tuning argument collapses. Meyer responds that Stenger's simulations use a simplistic definition of "life-permitting" and that the deeper the analysis goes, the more finely tuned the constants appear.

Sean Carroll, The Big Picture: On the Origins of Life, Meaning, and the Universe Itself (2016): Carroll, a Caltech physicist, argues that Meyer's three-part case conflates scientific evidence with philosophical interpretation at every step. On fine-tuning: the fact that our universe permits life does not itself require a designer — observers necessarily find themselves in life-permitting universes (the anthropic principle), so the observation carries no evidential weight independent of a prior probability assignment about universes. Carroll's deeper argument: the theistic hypothesis does not actually make predictions that differ from naturalism in any testable way. If God could create any kind of universe, theism is compatible with every possible observation — which means no observation constitutes evidence for theism over naturalism. On consciousness: Carroll defends "poetic naturalism" — the emergence of consciousness from matter is surprising but not logically impossible, and the hard problem of consciousness is a conceptual puzzle rather than an empirical gap requiring a supernatural explanation.

Richard Dawkins, The God Delusion (2006) — "Who Designed the Designer?": Dawkins's central philosophical objection to all design arguments: if the universe's complexity requires a designer, then the designer must be at least as complex as the universe, which means the designer requires a designer, producing an infinite regress. God, on this view, doesn't explain complexity — he relocates it. Meyer's response (which is a standard theistic response) is that God is not a complex physical entity requiring explanation but a necessary being who is simple in the philosophical sense — a mind, not a machine. This is the standard Thomistic response: God is actus purus, pure act, containing no unrealized potency and therefore no complexity in the relevant sense. Whether Dawkins's objection is fatal depends entirely on what kind of God is being posited — it works against some concepts of God and not others.

Lawrence Krauss, A Universe from Nothing (2012): Krauss argues that quantum physics has undermined the cosmological argument's premise that "something cannot come from nothing" — virtual particles appear and disappear from the quantum vacuum constantly, and the entire observable universe could have arisen from a quantum fluctuation in a pre-existing quantum vacuum. Meyer and philosopher David Albert both respond that Krauss's "nothing" is not actual nothing — a quantum vacuum is a rich physical entity with properties, laws, and energy. The debate between Meyer and Krauss is partly terminological (what counts as "nothing?") and partly substantive (can physical law itself have a cause?). Albert's review in the New York Times called Krauss's argument a "sleight of hand" — the objection is substantive regardless of one's theology.

Critical Assessment

The most philosophically sophisticated of Meyer's three books, and the most directly relevant to Catholic natural theology. The fine-tuning argument is taken seriously by physicists who are not ID proponents — Paul Davies, Martin Rees, and Roger Penrose have all written about fine-tuning as a genuine puzzle without endorsing ID specifically. Meyer's cumulative case approach is methodologically strong — the three arguments are genuinely independent. The weakest section is consciousness, where the philosophical argument is real but the connection to intelligent design (as opposed to theism generally) is less clear. The strongest version of the critical response: the God hypothesis doesn't actually explain fine-tuning — it relocates the explanation rather than providing one.

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Catholicism and Science, Lemaître lecture, Five Ways, Teilhard and the Omega Point, theodicy). The most direct scientific engagement with the cosmological and teleological arguments for God's existence in the course reading list.

Notable Quote
"The evidence from physics, cosmology, and biology converges on the same startling conclusion: the universe gives every indication of having been designed by a prior intelligence."— Stephen C. Meyer, Return of the God Hypothesis (2021)
Actionable Takeaways
  • The three-part framework (cosmology, biology, consciousness) gives you independent lines of evidence to deploy separately in different conversations.
  • The multiverse response to fine-tuning is not as strong as it sounds — learn why before encountering it in debate.
  • Read alongside a sympathetic mainstream physicist treatment of fine-tuning (Paul Davies's The Cosmic Jackpot) and a skeptical response (Victor Stenger's The Fallacy of Fine-Tuning) for a full picture.
Central Claim

Theistic evolution — the position that God used unguided Darwinian evolution to create life, including human beings — is scientifically inadequate, philosophically incoherent, and theologically problematic for orthodox Christianity. This is a multi-author critique of the BioLogos position (associated with Francis Collins and the evolutionary creationism movement).

Supporting Arguments

Scientific Critique (Meyer, Behe, Axe, Gauger): Standard Neo-Darwinian mechanisms are insufficient to produce the new biological information required for macroevolution — the same arguments made in Meyer's solo books, applied specifically to theistic evolution's acceptance of unguided evolution.

Philosophical Critique (Moreland, Meyer): Theistic evolutionists typically claim God directed the evolutionary process while simultaneously affirming that the process was unguided — this is incoherent. If God directed it, it wasn't unguided; if it was unguided, God didn't direct it. The attempt to have both simultaneously fails philosophically.

Theological Critique (Grudem, Collins, Madueme, Williams): Theistic evolution, as typically formulated, requires revising or abandoning: (a) the historical Adam and Eve as the first humans and progenitors of the species; (b) the Fall as a historical event with real moral and physical consequences; (c) the special creation of human beings in the image of God; (d) death as a consequence of sin (Paul's argument in Romans 5 requires a pre-sin deathless world). These revisions have cascading consequences for the doctrines of original sin, atonement, and redemption.

Key Assumptions

The contributors assume that the relevant theistic evolutionists (BioLogos) mean genuinely unguided evolution, not front-loaded design. The theological section assumes a high view of biblical inerrancy. Many Catholic contributors to the broader TE debate would not share these assumptions.

Counterarguments Addressed

Engages directly with Francis Collins (The Language of God), Denis Alexander (Creation or Evolution: Do We Have to Choose?), and Karl Giberson. The book was specifically motivated by perceived inadequacies in existing TE responses to ID critiques.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Francis Collins, The Language of God: A Scientist Presents Evidence for Belief (2006): Collins, director of the Human Genome Project and later of the NIH, makes the most prominent case for theistic evolution from an evangelical Christian perspective. His central argument: the genome is "the language of God" — its complexity and elegance reveal a Creator, but the mechanism of creation was evolutionary. Collins argues that the moral law (the human sense of altruism and right and wrong that exceeds what evolution would predict) and the fine-tuning of the universe are the genuine evidences for God — not biological complexity, which evolution explains perfectly well. On the origin question that Meyer raises, Collins argues the Meyer/Dembski critique of natural selection misunderstands how evolution actually works: evolution does not require each step to be independently functional at its intermediate stages, because neutral mutations can accumulate without selection until a functional threshold is reached. Collins founded BioLogos in 2007 specifically to provide a home for Christians who accept evolutionary science without abandoning orthodox faith.

Denis Alexander, Creation or Evolution: Do We Have to Choose? (2008): Alexander, a molecular biologist and former director of the Faraday Institute at Cambridge, argues for what he calls "Model A" theistic evolution: God created by and through the evolutionary process, using it as his instrument. Alexander directly rebuts the theological critique in Theistic Evolution — that TE requires abandoning a historical Adam — by arguing that "Adam and Eve" can be read as the first two representatives of Homo sapiens on whom God conferred spiritual status, without requiring them to be the sole progenitors of all humanity. This "federal headship" reading preserves the theological function of the Fall (the introduction of sin into human history through a real historical act of disobedience) without requiring a genetic bottleneck of two individuals that population genetics rules out. Alexander's primary audience is British evangelical scientists, and his theological engagement is more sophisticated than most American TE treatments.

Darrel Falk, Coming to Peace with Science (2004) and the BioLogos Response: Falk, a geneticist and former BioLogos president, argues that the theological objections in Theistic Evolution — especially the charge that TE makes God's action undetectable and therefore meaningless — misunderstand how God acts through secondary causes. Falk appeals directly to the Thomistic framework: God is the primary cause of evolution, working through natural secondary causes without those causes being miraculous interventions detectable to science. This is not "deistic evolution" (God set things up and stepped away) but genuine theistic evolution in which God is the continuous ground of being of the entire process. The BioLogos response to the incoherence objection is precisely the Catholic response: the question of whether evolution is "guided" or "unguided" is a metaphysical question that science cannot answer, and claiming evolution is "unguided" at the scientific level is compatible with claiming it is "guided" at the theological level, because they are describing the same event from different levels of analysis.

Critical Assessment

This is a polemical volume — its purpose is to critique, not survey. It should be read alongside BioLogos responses for balance. Important note for Catholic readers: The Catholic tradition's approach to evolution — Aquinas's primary/secondary causation framework — is actually more sophisticated than what most of this book's contributors offer. The Catholic position can affirm real evolutionary processes as secondary causes while maintaining God as primary cause, without the incoherence the contributors allege, because it doesn't require God's "intervention" to be detectable at the secondary level. Many Catholic scientists and theologians (including the Vatican Observatory's staff) hold theistic evolution positions that don't concede the theological problems the contributors identify.

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Catholicism and Science, primary/secondary causation, Lemaître, the Teilhard de Chardin lecture (Week 3). The Thomistic framework discussed in the Lemaître lecture is the Catholic answer to the incoherence objection that this book levels at theistic evolution.

Actionable Takeaways
  • The incoherence objection (you can't simultaneously affirm unguided evolution and say God directed it) is philosophically serious — theistic evolutionists need to answer it.
  • The Thomistic primary/secondary causation framework is the Catholic answer: God causes truly through natural causes, so the causation is genuinely "unguided" at the secondary level while being divinely directed at the primary level.
  • Read alongside BioLogos responses (Denis Alexander, Darrel Falk) and the Vatican Observatory's writings for the Catholic position.
Central Claim

Science and Christian faith not only can coexist — specific scientific findings in cosmology, biology, archaeology, and neuroscience actively support Judeo-Christian belief. This is a multi-author reference volume (45+ contributors) with a foreword by Meyer, organized as an encyclopedic treatment of science-faith questions from an intelligent design perspective.

Key Note on Authorship

This is not a Stephen C. Meyer solo work. Primary editors are William Dembski, Casey Luskin, and Joseph Holden. Meyer wrote the Foreword and is among the contributors. It is included here because it is frequently associated with Meyer and provides the broadest single-volume treatment of the ID research program.

Structure

Four sections: (1) Science and Faith — compatibility arguments, historical relationship, philosophical foundations; (2) Science and Design — the ID case across multiple domains; (3) Science and Evolution — critique of evolutionary theory; (4) Hard Questions — archaeology and Scripture, consciousness, fine-tuning, and specific apologetic issues. Contributors include Michael Behe, Douglas Axe, Jonathan Wells, Guillermo Gonzalez, Jay Richards, and others.

Critical Assessment

As a reference volume, the quality varies by chapter and contributor. Its chief value is breadth — it surveys more of the science-faith terrain than any of Meyer's individual books. It is stronger as a one-stop reference to find arguments on a specific topic than as a sustained argument to read cover to cover. The consistent ID-friendly perspective means alternative positions are often characterized rather than engaged at full strength. Useful as a reference alongside more mainstream science-faith resources (e.g., Ian Barbour's four-model framework, the Vatican Observatory's publications).

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Catholicism and Science), and the Thaller/JRE discussion of science's limits. Best used as a reference when a specific science-faith question arises.

Actionable Takeaways
  • Use this as a reference, not a cover-to-cover read.
  • The section on historical relationships between science and Christianity (correcting the "warfare thesis") is particularly strong and accessible.
  • Note that the volume represents one coherent perspective (Discovery Institute / ID) — supplement with Ian Barbour or the Vatican Observatory for Catholic perspectives that differ on evolution.

Paul Copan

Central Claim

The God of the Old Testament — despite the portrait drawn by Richard Dawkins, Christopher Hitchens, and Sam Harris — is not an arbitrary, genocidal tyrant. The OT commands that trouble modern readers (Canaanite genocide, slavery, treatment of women, animal sacrifice) can be understood in their historical, literary, and theological context in ways that are morally defensible and in fact reveal a God more humane than his ancient contemporaries.

Supporting Arguments

Ancient Near Eastern Context: Israelite law compared to contemporary law codes (Hammurabi, Hittite, Assyrian) is significantly more humane — protections for slaves that no surrounding culture provided, limits on violence, provisions for the poor, rights for resident aliens. The charge that Mosaic law is barbaric ignores that it was operating in and gradually improving a Bronze Age context.

The Hyperbole Argument: The "utterly destroy" language in Joshua and Deuteronomy (herem warfare) is standard Ancient Near Eastern military rhetoric — the same hyperbolic victory language appears in Egyptian, Assyrian, and Moabite inscriptions and clearly doesn't mean literal killing of every person. The same texts that command destruction show Canaanites still living in the land afterward, confirming the commands weren't literally carried out as described.

Progressive Revelation: God accommodated his commands to the moral capacity of the Israelite people at a particular stage of their history. The slave laws, divorce provisions, and other concessions Moses permitted are not God's ideal — Jesus himself says this about divorce ("from the beginning it was not so" — Matthew 19:8). The trajectory of the biblical narrative points toward a higher standard consistently reached in the New Testament.

Specific Cases Examined: Slavery (debt-bondage vs. chattel slavery — very different institutions); treatment of women (Israelite women had more rights than most ANE contemporaries); the Canaanite "genocide" (directed at combatants in specific cities, not civilians throughout the region; Canaanites could and did leave).

Key Assumptions

The ANE context is the appropriate interpretive frame. Hyperbolic military rhetoric was a recognized convention, not deceptive. Progressive revelation is a coherent theological concept that allows for moral development across the testaments.

Counterarguments Addressed

Engages Dawkins (The God Delusion), Hitchens (God Is Not Great), and Harris (The End of Faith) directly. Also addresses the "divine command theory" objection (is something good merely because God commands it?) via the Euthyphro dilemma. Engages evangelical critics who think the hyperbole argument undermines biblical authority.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Richard Dawkins, The God Delusion, Chapter 2 (2006): Dawkins's "rogues' gallery" of OT divine actions — commanding genocide of the Canaanites, authorizing slavery, ordering the killing of Sabbath-breakers, demanding the execution of disobedient children — represents the sharpest popular-level statement of the case Copan is responding to. Dawkins's specific claim is not merely that the OT God behaved badly but that the behavior is logically inconsistent with the omnibenevolent God Christianity claims to worship — you cannot have both a genocide-commanding God and an all-loving God; the concepts contradict. Dawkins further argues that Copan-style responses ("context," "hyperbole," "progressive revelation") are motivated reasoning — the same hermeneutical moves would be condemned if applied to any other ancient text by religious apologists defending their own tradition. The honest response, Dawkins argues, is to admit the OT God is a moral monster and either abandon theism or admit the Bible is not morally authoritative.

Christopher Hitchens, God Is Not Great: How Religion Poisons Everything (2007): Hitchens's argument is structurally different from Dawkins's and harder for Copan to address: Hitchens is less interested in specific OT texts than in the general pattern of religiously authorized violence across history. Even if Copan successfully explains the Canaanite passages as hyperbole or context, Hitchens argues the historical record of actual Church behavior (Crusades, Inquisition, support for colonial violence, complicity in genocide) demonstrates that religious moral authority consistently produces moral catastrophe. The Bible's specific problems are downstream of the larger problem: that any tradition claiming divine authority for moral commands produces followers who will commit atrocities in the name of those commands. Copan's book-level response is largely to the text-critical arguments; the historical argument Hitchens makes is addressed more directly in Copan's later work but is never fully answered at the book level.

Eric Seibert, Disturbing Divine Behavior: Troubling Old Testament Images of God (2009): Seibert is a Christian OT scholar who argues Copan's approach — defending the OT texts as consistent with a good God — is both exegetically wrong and pastorally harmful. Seibert argues that the texts really do portray God commanding genocide, and that the appropriate Christian response is not to explain this away but to acknowledge that the human authors of Scripture got God wrong in these passages — that their portraits of God are distorted by their own cultural assumptions, military ideologies, and limited moral vision. On this view, Scripture is authoritative not because every portrait of God in it is accurate but because it traces a trajectory of progressive revelation in which the human understanding of God's character grows over time, culminating in Jesus, who is the definitive revelation of who God is. Seibert's critique of Copan is that the apologetic impulse to defend every biblical text produces a portrait of God that is less morally serious than the honest admission that some texts got him wrong.

Critical Assessment

This is one of the best popular-level responses to the new atheist critique of the OT. The ANE context argument is well-established in mainstream scholarship and not invented by ID apologists. The hyperbole argument is more contested — some scholars (both secular and Christian) find it reads too much into the texts, and some evangelical critics worry it opens the door to reading away other difficult passages. The slavery chapter requires careful reading: Copan's distinction between OT debt-bondage and American chattel slavery is legitimate but can feel like special pleading when the mistreatment of slaves in OT texts is examined in detail. Copan's overall approach is honest — he doesn't pretend the texts aren't challenging.

Connections to the Course

Week 8 (Antisemitism and the Church's historical relationship with Judaism, understanding why the OT world matters), Week 3 (Problem of Evil and theodicy). The Course's Week 2 lecture on Jewish roots of Catholic worship is better understood after reading Copan — the God who commanded the Canaanite wars is the same God whose liturgical patterns Catholics inherit.

Notable Quote
"The God of the Old Testament is often more gracious and less violent than his critics suggest — and the violence that does appear is set in a context that the new atheists systematically ignore."— Paul Copan, Is God a Moral Monster? (2011)
Actionable Takeaways
  • Learn the ANE context before defending the OT — knowing that Hammurabi's Code allows masters to kill slaves while Mosaic law does not changes the conversation.
  • The trajectory of Scripture matters as much as individual commands — the direction is consistently toward more dignity, more mercy, more universalism.
  • When someone cites the OT God as a reason not to believe, this is the book to have read.
Central Claim

The apparent tension between the "wrathful" God of the Old Testament and the "loving" God of the New Testament is a false dichotomy — both testaments present the same God with the same character. Readings that construct an OT monster and an NT softie misread both testaments. The OT is more gracious than critics admit; the NT is more demanding and judgment-oriented than progressive Christianity acknowledges.

Supporting Arguments

The OT's Gracious God: The OT is saturated with mercy, covenant love (hesed), patience, and forgiveness. God delays judgment for generations, pleads with Israel through the prophets, weeps over Jerusalem. The "wrath" passages exist alongside and are framed by abundant mercy. The God who commands the Canaanite wars is the same God who says "I take no pleasure in the death of the wicked" (Ezekiel 33:11).

The NT's Demanding God: Jesus speaks more about hell than any other figure in the NT. The book of Revelation presents the most graphic divine judgment imagery in all of Scripture. The NT's God is not softer — the demands are higher, the stakes are clearer (eternal rather than temporal consequences), and the judgment is more universal. Progressive Christianity's selective NT reading misses this consistently.

Against Marcionism: The heresy of Marcion (2nd century) — that the OT God is a lesser, malevolent deity different from the NT's loving Father — was the first major Christian heresy. Its modern equivalents (in progressive Christianity's dismissal of the OT God and in the new atheism's selective reading) make the same fundamental error.

NT Difficult Passages: Examines specific NT passages often cited as showing divine vindictiveness — the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira (Acts 5), eternal punishment imagery, the book of Revelation's violence, and Paul's "delivered to Satan" passages — and provides contextual interpretation of each.

Key Assumptions

The Bible speaks with a unified theological voice across testaments. Marcion's error is not just ancient — it shapes much contemporary reading. The same hermeneutical principles that work for OT difficult passages apply to NT ones.

Critical Assessment

A natural sequel to Is God a Moral Monster? that covers territory the first book left incomplete. Particularly valuable for the NT difficult passages chapter, which has no direct equivalent in evangelical apologetics. The section on eternal punishment will not satisfy universalists — Copan defends a traditional view of hell as eternal conscious punishment while acknowledging the tradition's inner tensions. Some reviewers find the second book more repetitive of the first than genuinely new; it is best read as a complement rather than a replacement. The Marcion chapter is the most historically important — the heresy it addresses is more widespread than most Catholics realize.

Connections to the Course

Week 3 (Death and Afterlife, hell), Week 8 (Antisemitism — the Marcionite roots of Christian anti-Judaism), the apophatic theology lecture (the God who exceeds our categories is also the God who judges).

Actionable Takeaways
  • Read Hosea 11 ("When Israel was a child I loved him...") alongside Revelation 19 (the rider on the white horse) — the same God is speaking in both. Understanding this prevents both sentimentality and severity.
  • Learn what Marcionism is and learn to recognize it — it's the most common theological error in progressive Christianity and in the culture at large.
  • Read this after Is God a Moral Monster? — they form a pair.
Central Claim

The biblical commands to "utterly destroy" the Canaanites (the herem warfare passages in Deuteronomy and Joshua) do not describe or mandate literal genocide of civilians, and can be understood — through careful attention to ANE literary conventions, internal biblical evidence, and moral philosophy — in ways consistent with a morally good God.

Supporting Arguments

The Hyperbole Argument (Primary): The "utterly destroy, leave nothing alive" language is standard Ancient Near Eastern military rhetoric — the same hyperbolic victory formula appears in the Merneptah Stele (Egyptian), Mesha Stele (Moabite), and Assyrian annals. In these texts it clearly doesn't mean literal killing of every person; it is a conventional claim of total victory. Applying this recognized literary convention to the Joshua texts explains why the same accounts that "utterly destroy" the Canaanites show them still very much present in subsequent chapters.

Internal Biblical Evidence: Joshua 10–11 use the herem formula repeatedly, but subsequent chapters (Judges 1) show Canaanites still living throughout the land. Either the herem commands were not carried out (in which case they were hyperbolic imperatives, not genocide commands) or the texts contradict themselves (the hyperbole reading is more coherent).

Who Was Actually Targeted: The commands target specific city-states and their military populations, not the entire Canaanite ethnic group throughout the region. Non-combatants could and did leave (Rahab's family; the Gibeonite treaty shows that surrender was an option). This is not ethnic cleansing of a people but military engagement with specific polities.

Philosophical Defense: Even granting a literal reading for the sake of argument, Copan and Flannagan argue that a specific divine command authorizing a particular historical action doesn't generate a general principle applicable to all times and places. God can authorize what would otherwise be impermissible — analogous to how a lawful authority can authorize actions that would be illegal without that authority (capital punishment, war).

Key Assumptions

ANE hyperbolic conventions apply to these texts. The internal biblical evidence (Canaanites still present after the "destruction") is meant to be read at face value rather than as a contradiction. Divine command theory has a coherent philosophical defense.

Counterarguments Addressed

Engages philosopher Wes Morriston's objections to divine command theory. Addresses Hector Avalos's secular critique. Engages evangelical critics who argue the hyperbole reading undermines biblical inerrancy. Also addresses the "if God commanded it, isn't it by definition good?" (Euthyphro) objection with Flannagan's modified divine command theory.

The Counterargument Case — From the Other Side

Wes Morriston, "Did God Command Genocide? A Challenge to the Biblical Inerrantist," Philosophia Christi (2009): Morriston's philosophical objection is the most technically rigorous challenge to Copan and Flannagan's divine command theory defense. His argument: even granting that God could authorize actions normally impermissible, the specific claim — that God commanded the killing of Canaanite infants and children — cannot be morally justified by any framework that also claims God is perfectly good. If we say "God commanded it, therefore it was just," we have made God's commands the definition of justice rather than a consequence of it, which means justice has no content independent of God's will — making the claim "God is just" tautologically empty. If we say "God commanded it because it was just," we must be able to give an independent account of why the killing of infants is just — which Copan and Flannagan cannot do without invoking the very moral framework they claim God's command supersedes. Morriston argues the divine command defense is philosophically incoherent: it either empties justice of content or requires a moral justification for infant killing that doesn't exist.

Hector Avalos, The End of Biblical Studies (2007): Avalos, a secular biblical scholar, argues from a different direction: that the entire project of making the Bible morally relevant to contemporary life is misguided, because the Bible's moral framework is so thoroughly embedded in ancient Near Eastern assumptions (covenant obligation, collective punishment, ethnic election) that no amount of contextualizing or reinterpreting makes it a suitable guide for modern ethics. On the genocide passages specifically, Avalos argues the hyperbole reading is not supported by the biblical authors' own self-understanding — they believed they were recording divine commands that were literally obeyed. The later historiographical reflection that shows Canaanites still in the land (Judges 1) is not evidence that Joshua's commands were figurative; it is evidence that Israel failed to carry out what it believed was a genuine divine mandate. Avalos's challenge to Copan is fundamentally about authorial intent: the texts mean what their authors intended, and their authors intended a literal genocide command.

The Moral Intuition Problem: Perhaps the deepest objection Copan and Flannagan face — articulated across multiple critics — is what philosophers call the "moral intuition problem." Both authors ultimately appeal to the claim that our moral intuitions (that killing innocent children is wrong) can be overridden by divine command. But our moral intuitions are also the source of our belief that God is good — if they're reliable enough to ground theism, they're reliable enough to condemn the Canaanite passages as morally monstrous. C.S. Lewis himself recognized this problem: if God commanded something our moral intuitions recognize as evil, we would have more reason to doubt the revelation than to abandon the intuition. Some Christian philosophers (including Lewis and Alvin Plantinga) have argued this makes divine command theory of the Copan/Flannagan variety self-undermining.

Critical Assessment

The most academically rigorous of the three Copan volumes — co-authored with philosopher Matthew Flannagan, whose contributions give the philosophical sections significantly more depth than Is God a Moral Monster? The hyperbole argument is supported by significant ANE scholarship (Nicholas Wolterstorff, John Goldingay, and others outside the apologetics world). Critics on the secular left find the argument apologetically motivated. Critics on the evangelical right worry it concedes too much to historical-critical methodology. The most honest assessment: the hyperbole reading is a genuine scholarly position supported by mainstream ANE scholarship, not an apologetic invention — but it is not the only scholarly reading, and the philosophical divine command section is more controversial than the literary analysis section.

Connections to the Course

Week 8 (Antisemitism and the Church's relationship with Judaism — why Christians need to understand the OT on its own terms), Week 3 (Problem of Evil). The most focused and scholarly treatment of the single most common intellectual objection to the OT in the course reading list.

Notable Quote
"The herem texts, read in their literary and historical context, look much less like commands to exterminate civilians and much more like the hyperbolic victory rhetoric found throughout the ancient Near East — rhetoric whose purpose was proclamation of divine triumph, not literal description of killing."— Paul Copan and Matthew Flannagan, Did God Really Command Genocide? (2014)
Actionable Takeaways
  • Learn the Merneptah Stele and the Mesha Stele — they provide the ANE parallel texts that make the hyperbole argument concrete rather than speculative.
  • The question "did the Canaanites still live in the land after the 'destruction'?" is the single most important question to ask when reading Joshua — the text itself answers it.
  • Read this before any serious apologetic conversation about the OT — it's the most specific and scholarly available response to the most specific and serious objection.

Anthony DeStefano

Publisher: Doubleday (Penguin Random House) · Genre: Popular Christian spirituality · Audience: General Christian readership across denominations · Note: This book preceded and is entirely independent of the "Ten Prayers" section in Appendix B of this course, which was written separately from scratch using Scripture and the Catechism as primary sources. The overlap in concept is real; the content and framing differ significantly.

Central Claim

Most people's prayer lives are frustrated because they spend their energy on prayers that don't always work — petitions for specific outcomes God may or may not grant — while neglecting a category of prayer that God always answers. These ten prayers, rooted in Scripture, address fundamental human needs in ways so aligned with God's will that his response is guaranteed. Prayer is fundamentally communication between the human person and God, and communication — once truly initiated — always gets a response.

The Ten Prayers

1. "God, show me that You exist." DeStefano opens here deliberately — if God's existence is in doubt, none of the other prayers make sense. He argues that an honest, open-hearted request to know whether God is real is always answered, because God desires to be known and responds to sincere seeking. This prayer is the entry point for those who are skeptical or lapsed.

2. "God, make me an instrument." The prayer to be used by God — to have one's life directed toward purposes beyond oneself. DeStefano frames this as one of the most counterintuitive prayers in the book: we typically ask God to serve our purposes; this prayer inverts the request. It is always answered because it asks for the exact role God created us to fill.

3. "God, outdo me in generosity." A prayer of financial and material trust — asking God to be more generous with you than you are with others. DeStefano grounds this in the biblical principle that generous giving opens the way for God's provision. This is the most practically daring prayer in the book and the one most likely to be misread as prosperity gospel; DeStefano addresses this by grounding it in sacrifice and trust rather than entitlement.

4. "God, get me through this suffering." Not a prayer to remove suffering but to be sustained through it. DeStefano argues this is one of the most reliably answered prayers precisely because God does not promise to remove suffering — he promises to be present in it and to bring good from it. The prayer reframes the request: from "take this away" to "carry me through."

5. "God, forgive me." The prayer of genuine contrition. DeStefano argues that God's forgiveness is never withheld from the truly repentant, grounding this in the parable of the Prodigal Son and the consistent biblical pattern of mercy responding to repentance. He is careful to distinguish sincere sorrow from mere regret about consequences.

6. "God, give me peace." Not the peace the world gives (absence of conflict or anxiety) but the peace Christ promised — a settled confidence in God's sovereignty that coexists with difficulty. DeStefano ties this to the Philippians 4:7 "peace that surpasses understanding" and argues it is always available to those who bring their anxieties to God rather than managing them alone.

7. "God, give me courage." The prayer for the strength to do what is right when it is costly. DeStefano connects this to the consistent biblical pattern of God strengthening those he calls — Joshua, David, the apostles — and argues that courage is always given to those who ask because God never calls without equipping.

8. "God, give me wisdom." Directly from James 1:5 — the explicit biblical promise that wisdom is given generously to all who ask. DeStefano treats this as one of the clearest unconditional promises in Scripture and the foundation for good decision-making in every area of life.

9. "God, bring good out of this bad situation." The prayer of Romans 8:28 — that God works all things together for good for those who love him. DeStefano argues this prayer is always answered, though not always in the way or timeframe we expect. The "good" God brings may be invisible for years and may look completely different from what we imagined.

10. "God, lead me to my destiny." The prayer to be guided toward the specific purpose God has for one's life. DeStefano argues that every person has a unique God-given destiny and that God always responds to the sincere request to be led toward it — though the path may involve detours, suffering, and surprises.

Key Assumptions

God desires a relationship of communication with every person regardless of their current belief or moral state — hence the book's appeal across denominational lines. The ten prayers work because they align with God's nature and his stated will; they are not techniques that work by virtue of their wording but dispositions of the heart. Prayer is not negotiating with God but opening oneself to what God is already trying to give.

Counterarguments Addressed

DeStefano directly addresses the most common objection: "I prayed this way and nothing happened." His response distinguishes between the prayer being answered and the answer being recognizable on the pray-er's timeline.
