A companion is literally someone you share bread with. The Eucharist — shared bread — is the original companionship. The word entered English through the Church's communal meal tradition.

🕌 Cross-Religion Connections

Fact 25

Mary appears more times in the Quran than in the entire New Testament. She is the only woman named in the Quran (other women are referred to by relationship to men). The Quran has an entire chapter named after her (Surah Maryam, Chapter 19) and explicitly affirms the Virgin Birth of Jesus. More devout Muslims venerate Mary than many Christians realize.

Fact 26

The Mass follows the same basic structure as the ancient synagogue service. The Liturgy of the Word (Scripture readings + homily) mirrors the Torah reading + sermon of the synagogue. The Liturgy of the Eucharist derives from the Jewish Passover Seder and the Birkat Hamazon (grace after meals). The first Christians were Jews who adapted Jewish liturgical forms to proclaim the fulfillment of what those forms pointed toward.

Fact 27

Buddhism and Catholicism both use prayer beads (Buddhist mala beads / Catholic rosary), both have elaborate monastic traditions, both see suffering as spiritually significant rather than purely negative, and both have rich traditions of silent contemplative prayer. Thomas Merton (Trappist monk) spent the last years of his life in dialogue with Buddhist monks — he died in Bangkok in 1968, accidentally electrocuted by a faulty fan in his hotel room while attending an international conference on Asian Christian monasticism.

Fact 28

Both Buddhism and Catholicism teach that suffering has transformative spiritual potential. The Buddha's First Noble Truth: suffering (dukkha) is the fundamental condition of existence. The Catholic teaching: suffering united to Christ's can be redemptive (Colossians 1:24; Salvifici Doloris). The difference is significant — Buddhism seeks liberation from suffering through non-attachment; Catholicism seeks transformation through suffering united to love. Both traditions reject the modern assumption that all suffering is purely negative.

Fact 29

All three Abrahamic faiths (Judaism, Christianity, Islam) share the same three pillars of religious practice — and Jesus himself commended all three. Prayer, fasting, and almsgiving appear in Matthew 6:1–18 as the three foundational practices of the religious life (each introduced with "When you..."). Ramadan combines all three: intensive prayer, total daytime fasting, and dramatically increased charitable giving.

Fact 30

Islam and Catholicism both have formal daily prayer times. The Islamic five daily prayers (salat) and the Catholic Liturgy of the Hours (seven "hours": Matins, Lauds, Prime, Terce, Sext, None, Vespers, Compline) are parallel attempts to sanctify time — to mark the entire day with prayer rather than relegating prayer to a single weekly event. Both traditions understand time itself as something that must be hallowed.

Fact 31

Hinduism and Catholicism both use incense, bells, and water in worship — not because of mutual borrowing but because these elements arise naturally from similar intuitions about how material creation can mediate encounter with the sacred. Incense (prayer ascending to God), bells (calling attention to the sacred moment), and water (purification and renewal) appear across religious traditions that developed entirely independently.

Fact 32

"Amen," "Alleluia," and "Hosanna" are all Hebrew words used unchanged in every Catholic Mass in every language. A Catholic in Japan and a Catholic in Brazil both say "Amen" — the same word the ancient Israelites used in temple worship three thousand years ago. The Mass is the world's longest-running multilingual event that still uses the same words its founders used.

🌹 Mary — Surprising Facts

Fact 33

The oldest known Marian prayer dates to approximately 250 AD. The Sub Tuum Praesidium ("We fly to your patronage, O holy Mother of God...") was discovered on a Greek papyrus in Egypt in 1917 — the same year as the Fatima apparitions. Catholics have been praying to Mary for shelter and protection for seventeen centuries.

Fact 34

The Fatima solar miracle of October 13, 1917 was witnessed by an estimated 70,000 people — and reported in secular Portuguese newspapers, including anti-Catholic publications. The phenomenon (the sun appearing to "dance" or move toward the crowd) was reported by witnesses spread across a wide geographic area, ruling out a purely psychological explanation. The event is one of the most extensively documented alleged miracles in modern history.

Fact 35

The Image of Our Lady of Guadalupe (1531) has been studied extensively. Analysis has found: no brushstrokes visible under magnification; the image exists on only the surface fibers of the tilma with no underdrawing; the eyes contain what appear to be reflected images of the people present when Juan Diego unrolled the tilma before Bishop Zumárraga — as if the image captured what Mary was looking at in that moment. The tilma (a coarse cloak) has survived since 1531 without preservation treatment.

Fact 36

"Full of grace" in Luke 1:28 is a perfect passive participle in Greek (kecharitōmenē) — indicating a completed action with ongoing effects. Gabriel is not gracing Mary in that moment; he is addressing her as one who has been and remains graced. This grammatical detail is the primary scriptural basis for the doctrine of the Immaculate Conception — the belief that Mary was preserved from original sin from the moment of her conception.

Fact 37

The first half of the Hail Mary is entirely Scripture. "Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you" is Gabriel's greeting at the Annunciation (Luke 1:28). "Blessed are you among women and blessed is the fruit of your womb" is Elizabeth's greeting at the Visitation (Luke 1:42). Only the second half — "Holy Mary, Mother of God, pray for us sinners, now and at the hour of our death" — was added by the Church, reaching its current form by the 16th century.

⚡ Surprising History

Fact 38

The first written description of a Sunday Mass (150 AD) by Justin Martyr in his First Apology — written to the Roman Emperor to explain what Christians do — describes the same basic structure used today: Scripture readings, homily, prayers, bread and wine, distribution to those present. The Mass has been structurally stable for at least 1,876 years.

Fact 39

Vatican City only became an independent state in 1929. The Lateran Treaty, signed by Mussolini and Cardinal Gasparri, resolved the "Roman Question" that had existed since 1870, when Italian forces seized Rome and the Pope refused to recognize the new Italian state. For 59 years, every pope from Pius IX to Pius XI considered himself a "prisoner of the Vatican" and refused to leave its grounds.

Fact 40

Pope John Paul II survived an assassination attempt on May 13, 1981 — the anniversary of the first Fatima apparition. He credited Our Lady of Fatima with deflecting the bullet that would otherwise have killed him. He placed the bullet that struck him in the crown of the statue of Our Lady of Fatima at the Fatima shrine in Portugal, where it remains today.

Fact 41

The pretzel was invented as a Lenten food. Early medieval monks are said to have shaped unleavened dough into the form of arms crossed in prayer — the typical posture of early Christian prayer — and given them as rewards to children who learned their prayers. The word likely derives from the Latin bracchiola (little arms), from bracchium (arm), a reference to the crossed-arm shape. Whether this precise origin story is literally historical or a later legend, the pretzel's connection to Lenten practice is documented from at least the early medieval period.

Fact 42

The Psalms are the world's oldest continuously used prayer book. The 150 Psalms were composed between approximately 1000 BCE and 400 BCE — the earliest are nearly three thousand years old. Catholic priests pray the entire psalter every four weeks in the Liturgy of the Hours. Jesus prayed the psalms. They are the only texts shared between the daily prayer practice of Jews, Christians of every denomination, and — through the Quran's reference to the Zabur (Psalms of David) — Muslims.

Fact 43

The Sistine Chapel ceiling took Michelangelo only four years (1508–1512) — and he painted it largely alone, dismissing his assistants. He was 33 when he started and 37 when he finished. Despite the popular image, he did not lie on his back — he painted standing on scaffolding, arching his neck backward. He later wrote a sonnet complaining about the physical toll: "My beard toward Heaven, my memory I feel / Behind my nape, I have breast of a Harpy."

Fact 44

The Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom (Eastern Catholic and Orthodox Eucharistic rite) has been celebrated continuously since the 4th century in essentially its current form. It is simultaneously being celebrated right now in parishes from Greece to Ukraine to Lebanon to New York. The Eastern and Western liturgical traditions developed separately but from the same apostolic roots.

🎭 Famous Catholics You Might Not Know Were Catholic

Fact 45

J.R.R. Tolkien was a devout Catholic who credited the Eucharist as the source of his creative imagination. He wrote to his son Christopher: "Out of the darkness of my life, so much frustrated, I put before you the one great thing to love on earth: the Blessed Sacrament... There you will find romance, glory, honour, fidelity, and the true way of all your loves upon earth." He was also instrumental in C.S. Lewis's conversion to Christianity — though Lewis became Anglican rather than Catholic, a fact that disappointed Tolkien.

Fact 46

Blaise Pascal (1623–1662) — mathematician (Pascal's Triangle, probability theory, the first mechanical calculator, Pascal's Wager) was a devout Catholic who experienced a dramatic mystical conversion on November 23, 1654. He sewed a record of the experience into the lining of his jacket and wore it until his death. His Pensées remains one of the great works of Christian apologetics.

Fact 47

Alfred Hitchcock was a practicing Catholic who attended Mass regularly throughout his career in Hollywood. He described himself as "a Catholic in the cellar" — meaning deeply formed by Catholic guilt, fear, and moral seriousness without being publicly observant. The themes of sin, guilt, redemption, and confession run through virtually every major film: I Confess (1953) is literally about the seal of confession.

Fact 48

Dorothy Day (1897–1980) was a Communist youth, a journalist, had an abortion, lived unmarried with a man, and gave birth to a daughter — and then converted to Catholicism, founded the Catholic Worker Movement, spent 50 years in radical service to the poor, and is currently a candidate for sainthood. Her cause for beatification was formally opened in 2012. She is perhaps the most striking example in modern history of what conversion to Catholicism can actually look like.

Fact 49

St. Thomas More (1478–1535) — lawyer, humanist scholar, friend of Erasmus, author of Utopia, and Chancellor of England under Henry VIII — was beheaded for refusing to sign the Act of Supremacy making Henry head of the Church of England. His last words on the scaffold: "I die the King's good servant, and God's first." He is the patron saint of lawyers, politicians, and statesmen. Robert Bolt's 1960 play A Man for All Seasons (filmed 1966) is the classic dramatization.

Fact 50

G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) converted to Catholicism in 1922 after decades as an Anglican apologist. He is the author of the Father Brown detective stories (52 stories featuring a mild-mannered Catholic priest-detective who solves crimes by understanding sin). His conversion was prompted, he said, by the realization that the only explanation of the world that made complete sense was the Catholic one. He wrote to a friend: "The difficulty of explaining 'why I am a Catholic' is that there are ten thousand reasons all amounting to one reason: that Catholicism is true."

🍞 The Eucharist — Facts Worth Knowing

Fact 51

Perpetual Eucharistic adoration — where volunteers pray before the exposed Blessed Sacrament 24 hours a day — is maintained continuously in monasteries around the world. Some communities have maintained this without interruption for centuries. The largest perpetual adoration community in the United States has maintained continuous prayer before the Blessed Sacrament for decades without a single lapse.

Fact 52

At every Mass worldwide, the words of consecration are the same. In any of the approximately 3,000 languages in which Mass is celebrated, the priest speaks the words Jesus spoke at the Last Supper. A Catholic in Tokyo, Lagos, São Paulo, and Sacramento hears the same words on the same Sunday — from the same three-year lectionary cycle, with the same Eucharistic prayer. The universality is structural, not merely aspirational.

Fact 53

Ash Wednesday is the most-attended Catholic observance — more Catholics receive ashes than receive Communion on Christmas. In the United States, approximately 25 million people receive ashes on Ash Wednesday. The Catholic Church has never required Mass attendance on Ash Wednesday; it is not a holy day of obligation. People come because they want to.

Fact 54

The word "companion" literally means "with bread" (Latin com-panis). The deepest human friendships are built around shared meals — this is why the Eucharist, the shared meal of the Body of Christ, is called the sacrament of unity. You are most yourself — most fully a member of the Body — at the Eucharistic table. The meal is not a symbol of community; it constitutes the community by creating it.

➕
Suggest a fact: This appendix grows with the course. If you encounter something striking that belongs here — a historical fact, a surprising connection, a scientific discovery — note it and bring it to your OCIA discussions. The most interesting discoveries in Catholic formation often happen when someone asks the question nobody thought to ask before.
